0: Comparative cradle-to-grave life cycle assessment of biogas production from marine algae and cattle manure biorefineries
Adewale Giwa Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Masdar Institute of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 54224, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates
1:
<list>The impacts depend on the application of the biogas and sources of inputs. 
Process energy requirements and transportation of inputs contribute the largest impact share. 
The cattle manure biorefinery is slightly more eco-friendly when biogas is used for transportation.The macroalgae biorefinery becomes more eco- friendly when biogas is recycled.</list>

3: In the past, attention was directed towards the production of first generation biogas from crops that could otherwise be used for food production, leading to threats to food availability and decline in agricultural production (Allen et al., 2016; Guo et al., 2010). However, focus has now been shifted to the production of second and third generation biogas in recent times to avoid food- fuel-fiber competition. In addition, inherent local factors in some countries have necessitated the production of biogas from wastes and inedible materials (Chedid and Chaaban, 2003).

Like many other countries, there is increasing attention on bio- gas production in United Arab Emirates such that the biogas can be used as a substitute for fossil gas. The country is motivated by the need to select the biomass, among the potentially available ones in the territory, which would contribute lower impact to the environ-

ment (Bonk et al., 2015). There is a growing interest in the environ- mental friendly application of biogas, be it for transportation, heat or electricity production. Two biomass sources are assessed and compared in this paper marine macroalgae from algal bloom and cattle manure. These two biomass sources are selected because of their availability and relevance to the UAEs recent drive on biogas production as a source of clean energy (Sowunmi et al., 2015). Also, due to the need to consider the non-arable nature of the soil and arid climatic conditions in the United Arab Emirates, these biomass sources would not give rise to food-fuel-fiber crises (Mezher et al., 2012).

The UAEs desert land does not support growth of biomass crops in vast quantities but the macroalgae available in the country is unique because it can stand wide fluctuations in temperature between the summer and winter (John and Al-Thani, 2014). These native seaweeds can use saline sources of water that are abundant in UAE such as saline groundwater or seawater for growth (Samara et al., 2016). In the past, UAE has been susceptible to large-scale marine algal bloom (the 2008/2009 coral reef attack) which led

to the widespread degradation of the countrys eastern reefs in recent years (Grizzle et al., 2015). The abundant sunshine or solar irradiance in the country provides the propensity for the growth of macroalgae. The fast growth of macroalgae is also a good con- tributing factor for large-scale cultivation of macroalgae because the seaweeds can take up nutrients for eutrophic response (Liu et al., 2009). Macroalgae remove massive amounts of CO2 from the environment because they are phototrophs i.e. they grow with the aid of CO2 and nutrients in the presence of sunlight (Moreira and Pires, 2016). Although, studies on microalgae have overshad- owed the research on macroalgae in recent times because microal- gae grow more quickly and have more lipid content, macroalgae are easier to harvest because of their large multicellular sizes (Behera et al., 2014).

Likewise, animal wastes are abundant in the UAE because of the countrys large-scale investment in cattle and camel rearing. Most of the animal wastes, most especially animal manure, are washed down to the seas while a portion of the wastes is discharged to the wastewater treatment plants (Ashraf et al., 2016). However, the wastewater treatment plants do not have the capacity to treat high volumetric load of organic wastes. Eventually, this results into overloading and discharge of a significant portion of the waste into the sea (Giwa et al., 2015). Therefore, there is need to reuse the water in this bio-waste and convert the solid part to a useful pro- duct, i.e. biogas. There is need to reduce the pollutants emitted into the environment from the discharge of the organic waste into sea, including emissions from N20, SO2, and particulate matter which can cause adverse environmental effects and health problems.

This LCA is aimed at comparing the environmental conse- quences of all the stages in the cradle-to-grave life cycle of biogas produced from macroalgae and cattle manure. The LCA method defined within the standards ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 has been chosen as the appropriate framework for this assessment because the phases of goal and scope, inventory analysis, impact assess- ment, and interpretation of impacts are incorporated in these stan- dards (Finkbeiner, 2013). The method is implemented in the LCA software SimaPro version 7.3.3. Sensitivity analysis is also carried out to investigate the response of the assessed impacts to changes in the application of the biogas produced. Three scenarios are eval- uated. In Scenario 1, the biogas produced is considered to be used for electricity production, which is sent to the grid. The biogas pro- duced is considered to be used as transportation fuel in Scenario 2. In Scenario 3, the biogas produced is considered to be recycled back and used as the energy source for running the biorefineries, rather than grid-connected electricity.

From literature search, three previous LCAs of biofuel produc- tion from macroalgae have been carried out (Aresta et al., 2005; Evans and Wilkie, 2010; Langlois et al., 2012). Two of these studies (Aresta et al., 2005; Evans and Wilkie, 2010) quantified the impact of the energy produced from the process stage only, without assessing the environmental impacts for the entire life cycle. The entire life cycle includes the stages of cultivation, transportation, consumption of the biogas and waste disposal/reuse, apart from the processing stage. The third study (Langlois et al., 2012) coupled offshore wind production with seaweed production to make justi- fication for its eco-design without considering internal process design adjustments. In this study, the environmental impacts of the entire life cycle of biogas production from macroalgae biorefin- ery from the cultivation/transportation of the raw materials to the end of the product life are assessed. In addition, rather than cou- pling the biorefinery to external renewable energy sources which would increase the environmental footprints, the redesign of the biorefinery processes included internal adjustments only, such as product recycling. Also, this is the first cradle-to-grave LCA on bio- gas production from cattle manure. No other scientific investiga- tion on the cradle-to-grave LCA of biogas production from cattle

manure has been carried out before this study. There has been no previous attempt to also compare the LCA of two viable biomass resources for the production of biogas under arid environmental conditions.

Ahead of the Paris Agreement on Climate Change 2016, 186 countries including arid countries such as the UAE submitted plans detailing how they would reduce greenhouse gas pollution and cut global mean temperature to less than above pre-industrial rev- olution levels (Burki, 2017). Renewable energy is a significant com- ponent of these plans, of which biogas would play a remarkable role (Souza et al., 2017). However, some studies have indicated that biogas might be less environmentally friendly than fossil gas, unless (1) the biomass is carefully selected based on environ- mental conditions, and (2) a LCA that would incorporate all the stages in the entire life cycle is carried out (Lijoe et al., 2017; Wagner and Lewandowski, 2017). The two biomasses selected for this study are considered based on environmental conditions. In addition, a cradle-to-grave LCA has been carried out. This compar- ative LCA would be useful to government and environmental agen- cies, farmers and utility companies in their drive towards evaluating the environmental impacts of biogas production, most especially under arid conditions.

However, some studies have indicated that biogas might be less environmentally friendly than fossil gas, unless (1) the biomass is carefully selected based on environ- mental conditions, and (2) a LCA that would incorporate all the stages in the entire life cycle is carried out (Lijoe et al., 2017; Wagner and Lewandowski, 2017). The two biomasses selected for this study are considered based on environmental conditions. In addition, a cradle-to-grave LCA has been carried out. This compar- ative LCA would be useful to government and environmental agen- cies, farmers and utility companies in their drive towards evaluating the environmental impacts of biogas production, most especially under arid conditions.
7: In the past, attention was directed towards the production of first generation biogas from crops that could otherwise be used for food production, leading to threats to food availability and decline in agricultural production (Allen et al., 2016; Guo et al., 2010). However, focus has now been shifted to the production of second and third generation biogas in recent times to avoid food- fuel-fiber competition. In addition, inherent local factors in some countries have necessitated the production of biogas from wastes and inedible materials (Chedid and Chaaban, 2003).

Like many other countries, there is increasing attention on bio- gas production in United Arab Emirates such that the biogas can be used as a substitute for fossil gas. The country is motivated by the need to select the biomass, among the potentially available ones in the territory, which would contribute lower impact to the environ-

ment (Bonk et al., 2015). There is a growing interest in the environ- mental friendly application of biogas, be it for transportation, heat or electricity production. Two biomass sources are assessed and compared in this paper marine macroalgae from algal bloom and cattle manure. These two biomass sources are selected because of their availability and relevance to the UAEs recent drive on biogas production as a source of clean energy (Sowunmi et al., 2015). Also, due to the need to consider the non-arable nature of the soil and arid climatic conditions in the United Arab Emirates, these biomass sources would not give rise to food-fuel-fiber crises (Mezher et al., 2012).

The UAEs desert land does not support growth of biomass crops in vast quantities but the macroalgae available in the country is unique because it can stand wide fluctuations in temperature between the summer and winter (John and Al-Thani, 2014). These native seaweeds can use saline sources of water that are abundant in UAE such as saline groundwater or seawater for growth (Samara et al., 2016). In the past, UAE has been susceptible to large-scale marine algal bloom (the 2008/2009 coral reef attack) which led

to the widespread degradation of the countrys eastern reefs in recent years (Grizzle et al., 2015). The abundant sunshine or solar irradiance in the country provides the propensity for the growth of macroalgae. The fast growth of macroalgae is also a good con- tributing factor for large-scale cultivation of macroalgae because the seaweeds can take up nutrients for eutrophic response (Liu et al., 2009). Macroalgae remove massive amounts of CO2 from the environment because they are phototrophs i.e. they grow with the aid of CO2 and nutrients in the presence of sunlight (Moreira and Pires, 2016). Although, studies on microalgae have overshad- owed the research on macroalgae in recent times because microal- gae grow more quickly and have more lipid content, macroalgae are easier to harvest because of their large multicellular sizes (Behera et al., 2014).

Likewise, animal wastes are abundant in the UAE because of the countrys large-scale investment in cattle and camel rearing. Most of the animal wastes, most especially animal manure, are washed down to the seas while a portion of the wastes is discharged to the wastewater treatment plants (Ashraf et al., 2016). However, the wastewater treatment plants do not have the capacity to treat high volumetric load of organic wastes. Eventually, this results into overloading and discharge of a significant portion of the waste into the sea (Giwa et al., 2015). Therefore, there is need to reuse the water in this bio-waste and convert the solid part to a useful pro- duct, i.e. biogas. There is need to reduce the pollutants emitted into the environment from the discharge of the organic waste into sea, including emissions from N20, SO2, and particulate matter which can cause adverse environmental effects and health problems.

This LCA is aimed at comparing the environmental conse- quences of all the stages in the cradle-to-grave life cycle of biogas produced from macroalgae and cattle manure. The LCA method defined within the standards ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 has been chosen as the appropriate framework for this assessment because the phases of goal and scope, inventory analysis, impact assess- ment, and interpretation of impacts are incorporated in these stan- dards (Finkbeiner, 2013). The method is implemented in the LCA software SimaPro version 7.3.3. Sensitivity analysis is also carried out to investigate the response of the assessed impacts to changes in the application of the biogas produced. Three scenarios are eval- uated. In Scenario 1, the biogas produced is considered to be used for electricity production, which is sent to the grid. The biogas pro- duced is considered to be used as transportation fuel in Scenario 2. In Scenario 3, the biogas produced is considered to be recycled back and used as the energy source for running the biorefineries, rather than grid-connected electricity.

From literature search, three previous LCAs of biofuel produc- tion from macroalgae have been carried out (Aresta et al., 2005; Evans and Wilkie, 2010; Langlois et al., 2012). Two of these studies (Aresta et al., 2005; Evans and Wilkie, 2010) quantified the impact of the energy produced from the process stage only, without assessing the environmental impacts for the entire life cycle. The entire life cycle includes the stages of cultivation, transportation, consumption of the biogas and waste disposal/reuse, apart from the processing stage. The third study (Langlois et al., 2012) coupled offshore wind production with seaweed production to make justi- fication for its eco-design without considering internal process design adjustments. In this study, the environmental impacts of the entire life cycle of biogas production from macroalgae biorefin- ery from the cultivation/transportation of the raw materials to the end of the product life are assessed. In addition, rather than cou- pling the biorefinery to external renewable energy sources which would increase the environmental footprints, the redesign of the biorefinery processes included internal adjustments only, such as product recycling. Also, this is the first cradle-to-grave LCA on bio- gas production from cattle manure. No other scientific investiga- tion on the cradle-to-grave LCA of biogas production from cattle

manure has been carried out before this study. There has been no previous attempt to also compare the LCA of two viable biomass resources for the production of biogas under arid environmental conditions.

The LCA method defined within the standards ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 has been chosen as the appropriate framework for this assessment because the phases of goal and scope, inventory analysis, impact assess- ment, and interpretation of impacts are incorporated in these stan- dards (Finkbeiner, 2013). The method is implemented in the LCA software SimaPro version 7.3.3. Sensitivity analysis is also carried out to investigate the response of the assessed impacts to changes in the application of the biogas produced. Three scenarios are eval- uated. In Scenario 1, the biogas produced is considered to be used for electricity production, which is sent to the grid. The biogas pro- duced is considered to be used as transportation fuel in Scenario 2. In Scenario 3, the biogas produced is considered to be recycled back and used as the energy source for running the biorefineries, rather than grid-connected electricity.

From literature search, three previous LCAs of biofuel produc- tion from macroalgae have been carried out (Aresta et al., 2005; Evans and Wilkie, 2010; Langlois et al., 2012). Two of these studies (Aresta et al., 2005; Evans and Wilkie, 2010) quantified the impact of the energy produced from the process stage only, without assessing the environmental impacts for the entire life cycle. The entire life cycle includes the stages of cultivation, transportation, consumption of the biogas and waste disposal/reuse, apart from the processing stage. The third study (Langlois et al., 2012) coupled offshore wind production with seaweed production to make justi- fication for its eco-design without considering internal process design adjustments. In this study, the environmental impacts of the entire life cycle of biogas production from macroalgae biorefin- ery from the cultivation/transportation of the raw materials to the end of the product life are assessed. In addition, rather than cou- pling the biorefinery to external renewable energy sources which would increase the environmental footprints, the redesign of the biorefinery processes included internal adjustments only, such as product recycling. Also, this is the first cradle-to-grave LCA on bio- gas production from cattle manure. No other scientific investiga- tion on the cradle-to-grave LCA of biogas production from cattle

manure has been carried out before this study. There has been no previous attempt to also compare the LCA of two viable biomass resources for the production of biogas under arid environmental conditions.

Ahead of the Paris Agreement on Climate Change 2016, 186 countries including arid countries such as the UAE submitted plans detailing how they would reduce greenhouse gas pollution and cut global mean temperature to less than above pre-industrial rev- olution levels (Burki, 2017). Renewable energy is a significant com- ponent of these plans, of which biogas would play a remarkable role (Souza et al., 2017). However, some studies have indicated that biogas might be less environmentally friendly than fossil gas, unless (1) the biomass is carefully selected based on environ- mental conditions, and (2) a LCA that would incorporate all the stages in the entire life cycle is carried out (Lijoe et al., 2017; Wagner and Lewandowski, 2017). The two biomasses selected for this study are considered based on environmental conditions. In addition, a cradle-to-grave LCA has been carried out. This compar- ative LCA would be useful to government and environmental agen- cies, farmers and utility companies in their drive towards evaluating the environmental impacts of biogas production, most especially under arid conditions.
12: Two biogas plants are modeled one from macroalgae and the other from cattle manure. The functional unit is 1 GJ of biogas pro- duced per year. The life of each plant, from commissioning to decommissioning is considered to be 10 years. The geographical reference for the consumption of the biogas is UAE but some of the raw materials and capital goods required as inputs are obtained from other countries. The conceptual boundaries include the transportation of raw materials and capital goods into produc- tion sites; processing of raw materials into biogas; use of biogas for electricity production or transportation; and disposal of the waste sludge from the systems (Fig. 1). The impacts due to capital goods manufacturing, noise and odor are cut off in the boundaries. The impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 Hierarchist (EI 99 H) method.

The biogas produced from the anaerobic digestion of macroal- gae is composed of around 68% CH4, 31% CO2 and 1% H2S whereas that produced from cattle manure is composed of around 62% CH4, 37% CO2 and 1% H2S (Pilicka et al., 2011; Thy1/2 and Wenzel, 2007). As a result of this difference in biogas composition, macroalgae and cattle manure generate biogas with energy densities of 22 per m³ and 20 MJ per m³, respectively. A cradle-to-grave approach is considered, whereby all phases including: the trans- portation to, from and within the processing facility; raw materials processing; biogas use; and disposal of waste sludge by landfilling. In fact, an initial attempt was made to consider various options for reuse of the waste sludge by evaluating the impacts of the follow- ing possible sludge reuse approaches separately: (1) anaerobic digestion of the waste sludge, (2) direct production of electricity from waste sludge in microbial fuel cells, (3) incineration of the waste sludge with thermal energy recovery, (4) co-incineration of the waste sludge in coal-fired power plants, and (5) gasification and pyrolysis of the waste sludge. This preliminary investigation revealed that these approaches have more impact than landfilling because their energy requirements and process emissions out- weigh the environmental gains from sludge reuse. In addition, sec- ondary waste products would be obtained from these sludge reuse approaches, necessitating the need for further treatment (Hasan et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015).
Fig. 1. The conceptual boundaries of the life cycles of the biorefineries including the stages of transportation, cultivation (when required), processing, and waste disposal.

The most recent data are used for this assessment and it is con- sidered that the data will be viable for a period of 10 years based on the available technologies for biomass processing, biogas use, and waste sludge disposal. For biogas production from macroal- gae, credit is allocated to the CO2 absorbed during macroalgae cul- tivation. For biogas production from cattle manure, the environmental credit is allocated to the reuse of the manure, which would have otherwise been washed away to surrounding water bodies. Environmental credit is also allocated to the heat co-product obtained from the two biorefineries when the biogas is used for electricity production because this heat can be reused instead of depending on thermal energy from a natural gas-fired or diesel-fired boiler. The macroalgae biorefinery is equipped with an open pond and fed with saline water and sludge from a large- scale municipal wastewater treatment plant (Al Mafraq plant, Abu Dhabi) that supplies the required nutrients. The biorefinery is modeled to be located at a distance of 100 m from the wastewater plant.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis: (1) the internal walls of the open pond are made of ethylene propylenediene monomer (EPDM) rubber to prevent leaching of nutrients and salts to the ground; (2) initial algal biomass is first incubated in a protoplast incubator before it is fed to the pond. The protoplasts of Enteromorpha (Ulva) prolifera algae, a common green alga with tubular fronds and flattened branches, are obtained from the incubator; (3) macroalgal growth is ensured by CO2 from the exhaust from the petroleum refinery around the plant. CO2 from industrial sources such as petroleum refineries can increase macroalgal growth rate by a factor of 1.21.8; (4) the daily growth rate of macroalgae is estimated from the correlation or dependence of macroalgal growth on temperature variations, according to Eq. (1).

where DGR is daily growth rate of macrolgae and T is ambient tem- perature in Since macroalgal growth is also dependent on nutri- ent availability and salinity, the composition of the inlet sludge is considered to be constant; (5) the volume of sludge input required for the functional unit is 8.45 m³, i.e. 202 kg with 23% solid matter. In addition, the concentration of the saline water is kept at the aver- age salinity of Gulf water, i.e. 35,000 ppm. The algal biomass is then harvested and sent to a two-stage anaerobic digester for conversion

to biogas; (6) the total length of pipeline (galvanized steel pipes) considered for biogas processing is 109.2 km. A stainless steel dis- charge decanter (centrifuge) is used to dewater the harvested algae slurry before anaerobic digestion. The slurry is fed to the centrifuges basket, from where the solid phase goes to the inner wall under centrifugal force; (7) in the first stage of the two-stage digester, the feed is acidified at 38 for hydrolysis. After hydrolysis, the solid part is recycled to the first stage while the liquid part (effluent) is sent to the second stage. The second stage of digestion involves the use of a rotor to ensure that the effluent is exposed to a high concentration of methanogenic bacteria and conversion to biogas is optimized. 32.9 kg of free-flowing light brown odorless methano- genic bacteria powder is required to produce the functional unit; (8) the digestate from the digester is separated by a screw press into a solid part which is dried and landfilled, and a liquid part which is reused in the open pond for macroalgae cultivation. The screw press is a stainless steel automatic-running screw press used to dewater the sludge by gravity and the inner pressure is imparted by a grad- ually narrowed-pitch distance; (9) the biogas is stored in a spherical dual membrane tank made from steel fabric with a volume of 45.5 m³ and the location of biogas conversion to electricity is con- sidered to be 5 km away from the storage tank. The processing of macroalgae to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 2.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis of the cattle manure biorefinery: (1) the biorefinery is also con- sidered to be located at a distance of 100 m from Al Mafraq wastewater treatment plant; (2) the plant is equipped with a man- ure collection tank (a glass-lined steel tank) from where the cattle manure is sent to a homogenization tank for mixing; (3) the homogenization tank is equipped with a propeller-type mixer to facilitate the blending of the waste stream. The uniformly mixed waste is passed into an anaerobic digester where biogas is pro- duced; (4) in the anaerobic digester, the blended stream is con- verted to biogas by anaerobic bacteria (volatile suspended solids). The digester works optimally at neutral pH and mesophilic temperature of around 35 C. The carbon-nitrogen ratio of the feed for optimal biogas production should be in the standard range of 20:130:1 (Wang et al., 2012). In this study, the carbon-nitrogen ratio of 25:1 is used; (5) the digestate is then sent to a screw press where a liquid fraction is obtained and recycled back to the anaer- obic digester; (6) the solid part is dried and landfilled. The process- ing of cattle manure to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 3.
Fig. 2. Schematic of the processing of macroalgae to biogas.
Fig. 3. Schematic of the processing of cattle manure to biogas.

The input and output data for the processes in the two biore- fineries are obtained from open sources. They are then normalized to the functional unit. These inventory data are presented as mate- rial, energy and transportation requirements responsible for the emissions to the environment (land, water and air). The environ- mental loads involved in the manufacture of the capital goods are excluded. The inputs and outputs that are directly associated with the production and use of biogas, and disposal of the waste sludge are identified and quantified. The environmental impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 hierarchist method. The hierarchist method includes all substances that are deemed car- cinogenic based on international scientific consensus. The other

two possible methods that can be employed in Eco-indicator 99 LCA approach are the individualistic and egalitarian method. The hierarchist method is used in this impact assessment because the impacts are analyzed based on scientifically proven data. However, this method is further compared with the individualistic and legal- itarian views in the sensitivity analysis to see the influence of these views on the impacts.

In the Eco-indicator 99 approach, there are 11 impact (or mid- point) categories and 3 damage (or endpoint) categories. In this study, the impacts are assessed with respect to the impact cate- gories because the damage categories are also the reflection of the impact categories. The impact categories include the impacts due to carcinogens, respiratory organics, respiratory inorganics, cli- mate change, radiation, ozone layer, ecotoxicity, acidification and

Fig. 1. The conceptual boundaries of the life cycles of the biorefineries including the stages of transportation, cultivation (when required), processing, and waste disposal.

The most recent data are used for this assessment and it is con- sidered that the data will be viable for a period of 10 years based on the available technologies for biomass processing, biogas use, and waste sludge disposal. For biogas production from macroal- gae, credit is allocated to the CO2 absorbed during macroalgae cul- tivation. For biogas production from cattle manure, the environmental credit is allocated to the reuse of the manure, which would have otherwise been washed away to surrounding water bodies. Environmental credit is also allocated to the heat co-product obtained from the two biorefineries when the biogas is used for electricity production because this heat can be reused instead of depending on thermal energy from a natural gas-fired or diesel-fired boiler. The macroalgae biorefinery is equipped with an open pond and fed with saline water and sludge from a large- scale municipal wastewater treatment plant (Al Mafraq plant, Abu Dhabi) that supplies the required nutrients. The biorefinery is modeled to be located at a distance of 100 m from the wastewater plant.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis: (1) the internal walls of the open pond are made of ethylene propylenediene monomer (EPDM) rubber to prevent leaching of nutrients and salts to the ground; (2) initial algal biomass is first incubated in a protoplast incubator before it is fed to the pond. The protoplasts of Enteromorpha (Ulva) prolifera algae, a common green alga with tubular fronds and flattened branches, are obtained from the incubator; (3) macroalgal growth is ensured by CO2 from the exhaust from the petroleum refinery around the plant. CO2 from industrial sources such as petroleum refineries can increase macroalgal growth rate by a factor of 1.21.8; (4) the daily growth rate of macroalgae is estimated from the correlation or dependence of macroalgal growth on temperature variations, according to Eq. (1).

where DGR is daily growth rate of macrolgae and T is ambient tem- perature in Since macroalgal growth is also dependent on nutri- ent availability and salinity, the composition of the inlet sludge is considered to be constant; (5) the volume of sludge input required for the functional unit is 8.45 m³, i.e. 202 kg with 23% solid matter. In addition, the concentration of the saline water is kept at the aver- age salinity of Gulf water, i.e. 35,000 ppm. The algal biomass is then harvested and sent to a two-stage anaerobic digester for conversion

to biogas; (6) the total length of pipeline (galvanized steel pipes) considered for biogas processing is 109.2 km. A stainless steel dis- charge decanter (centrifuge) is used to dewater the harvested algae slurry before anaerobic digestion. The slurry is fed to the centrifuges basket, from where the solid phase goes to the inner wall under centrifugal force; (7) in the first stage of the two-stage digester, the feed is acidified at 38 for hydrolysis. After hydrolysis, the solid part is recycled to the first stage while the liquid part (effluent) is sent to the second stage. The second stage of digestion involves the use of a rotor to ensure that the effluent is exposed to a high concentration of methanogenic bacteria and conversion to biogas is optimized. 32.9 kg of free-flowing light brown odorless methano- genic bacteria powder is required to produce the functional unit; (8) the digestate from the digester is separated by a screw press into a solid part which is dried and landfilled, and a liquid part which is reused in the open pond for macroalgae cultivation. The screw press is a stainless steel automatic-running screw press used to dewater the sludge by gravity and the inner pressure is imparted by a grad- ually narrowed-pitch distance; (9) the biogas is stored in a spherical dual membrane tank made from steel fabric with a volume of 45.5 m³ and the location of biogas conversion to electricity is con- sidered to be 5 km away from the storage tank. The processing of macroalgae to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 2.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis of the cattle manure biorefinery: (1) the biorefinery is also con- sidered to be located at a distance of 100 m from Al Mafraq wastewater treatment plant; (2) the plant is equipped with a man- ure collection tank (a glass-lined steel tank) from where the cattle manure is sent to a homogenization tank for mixing; (3) the homogenization tank is equipped with a propeller-type mixer to facilitate the blending of the waste stream. The uniformly mixed waste is passed into an anaerobic digester where biogas is pro- duced; (4) in the anaerobic digester, the blended stream is con- verted to biogas by anaerobic bacteria (volatile suspended solids). The digester works optimally at neutral pH and mesophilic temperature of around 35 C. The carbon-nitrogen ratio of the feed for optimal biogas production should be in the standard range of 20:130:1 (Wang et al., 2012). In this study, the carbon-nitrogen ratio of 25:1 is used; (5) the digestate is then sent to a screw press where a liquid fraction is obtained and recycled back to the anaer- obic digester; (6) the solid part is dried and landfilled. The process- ing of cattle manure to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 3.
Fig. 2. Schematic of the processing of macroalgae to biogas.
Fig. 3. Schematic of the processing of cattle manure to biogas.

The input and output data for the processes in the two biore- fineries are obtained from open sources. They are then normalized to the functional unit. These inventory data are presented as mate- rial, energy and transportation requirements responsible for the emissions to the environment (land, water and air). The environ- mental loads involved in the manufacture of the capital goods are excluded. The inputs and outputs that are directly associated with the production and use of biogas, and disposal of the waste sludge are identified and quantified. The environmental impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 hierarchist method. The hierarchist method includes all substances that are deemed car- cinogenic based on international scientific consensus. The other

two possible methods that can be employed in Eco-indicator 99 LCA approach are the individualistic and egalitarian method. The hierarchist method is used in this impact assessment because the impacts are analyzed based on scientifically proven data. However, this method is further compared with the individualistic and legal- itarian views in the sensitivity analysis to see the influence of these views on the impacts.

In the Eco-indicator 99 approach, there are 11 impact (or mid- point) categories and 3 damage (or endpoint) categories. In this study, the impacts are assessed with respect to the impact cate- gories because the damage categories are also the reflection of the impact categories. The impact categories include the impacts due to carcinogens, respiratory organics, respiratory inorganics, cli- mate change, radiation, ozone layer, ecotoxicity, acidification and
16: Two biogas plants are modeled one from macroalgae and the other from cattle manure. The functional unit is 1 GJ of biogas pro- duced per year. The life of each plant, from commissioning to decommissioning is considered to be 10 years. The geographical reference for the consumption of the biogas is UAE but some of the raw materials and capital goods required as inputs are obtained from other countries. The conceptual boundaries include the transportation of raw materials and capital goods into produc- tion sites; processing of raw materials into biogas; use of biogas for electricity production or transportation; and disposal of the waste sludge from the systems (Fig. 1). The impacts due to capital goods manufacturing, noise and odor are cut off in the boundaries. The impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 Hierarchist (EI 99 H) method.

The biogas produced from the anaerobic digestion of macroal- gae is composed of around 68% CH4, 31% CO2 and 1% H2S whereas that produced from cattle manure is composed of around 62% CH4, 37% CO2 and 1% H2S (Pilicka et al., 2011; Thy1/2 and Wenzel, 2007). As a result of this difference in biogas composition, macroalgae and cattle manure generate biogas with energy densities of 22 per m³ and 20 MJ per m³, respectively. A cradle-to-grave approach is considered, whereby all phases including: the trans- portation to, from and within the processing facility; raw materials processing; biogas use; and disposal of waste sludge by landfilling. In fact, an initial attempt was made to consider various options for reuse of the waste sludge by evaluating the impacts of the follow- ing possible sludge reuse approaches separately: (1) anaerobic digestion of the waste sludge, (2) direct production of electricity from waste sludge in microbial fuel cells, (3) incineration of the waste sludge with thermal energy recovery, (4) co-incineration of the waste sludge in coal-fired power plants, and (5) gasification and pyrolysis of the waste sludge. This preliminary investigation revealed that these approaches have more impact than landfilling because their energy requirements and process emissions out- weigh the environmental gains from sludge reuse. In addition, sec- ondary waste products would be obtained from these sludge reuse approaches, necessitating the need for further treatment (Hasan et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015).

These inventory data are presented as mate- rial, energy and transportation requirements responsible for the emissions to the environment (land, water and air). The environ- mental loads involved in the manufacture of the capital goods are excluded. The inputs and outputs that are directly associated with the production and use of biogas, and disposal of the waste sludge are identified and quantified. The environmental impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 hierarchist method. The hierarchist method includes all substances that are deemed car- cinogenic based on international scientific consensus. The other

two possible methods that can be employed in Eco-indicator 99 LCA approach are the individualistic and egalitarian method. The hierarchist method is used in this impact assessment because the impacts are analyzed based on scientifically proven data. However, this method is further compared with the individualistic and legal- itarian views in the sensitivity analysis to see the influence of these views on the impacts.

In the Eco-indicator 99 approach, there are 11 impact (or mid- point) categories and 3 damage (or endpoint) categories. In this study, the impacts are assessed with respect to the impact cate- gories because the damage categories are also the reflection of the impact categories. The impact categories include the impacts due to carcinogens, respiratory organics, respiratory inorganics, cli- mate change, radiation, ozone layer, ecotoxicity, acidification and
19: Two biogas plants are modeled one from macroalgae and the other from cattle manure. The functional unit is 1 GJ of biogas pro- duced per year. The life of each plant, from commissioning to decommissioning is considered to be 10 years. The geographical reference for the consumption of the biogas is UAE but some of the raw materials and capital goods required as inputs are obtained from other countries. The conceptual boundaries include the transportation of raw materials and capital goods into produc- tion sites; processing of raw materials into biogas; use of biogas for electricity production or transportation; and disposal of the waste sludge from the systems (Fig. 1). The impacts due to capital goods manufacturing, noise and odor are cut off in the boundaries. The impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 Hierarchist (EI 99 H) method.

The biogas produced from the anaerobic digestion of macroal- gae is composed of around 68% CH4, 31% CO2 and 1% H2S whereas that produced from cattle manure is composed of around 62% CH4, 37% CO2 and 1% H2S (Pilicka et al., 2011; Thy1/2 and Wenzel, 2007). As a result of this difference in biogas composition, macroalgae and cattle manure generate biogas with energy densities of 22 per m³ and 20 MJ per m³, respectively. A cradle-to-grave approach is considered, whereby all phases including: the trans- portation to, from and within the processing facility; raw materials processing; biogas use; and disposal of waste sludge by landfilling. In fact, an initial attempt was made to consider various options for reuse of the waste sludge by evaluating the impacts of the follow- ing possible sludge reuse approaches separately: (1) anaerobic digestion of the waste sludge, (2) direct production of electricity from waste sludge in microbial fuel cells, (3) incineration of the waste sludge with thermal energy recovery, (4) co-incineration of the waste sludge in coal-fired power plants, and (5) gasification and pyrolysis of the waste sludge. This preliminary investigation revealed that these approaches have more impact than landfilling because their energy requirements and process emissions out- weigh the environmental gains from sludge reuse. In addition, sec- ondary waste products would be obtained from these sludge reuse approaches, necessitating the need for further treatment (Hasan et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015).
Fig. 1. The conceptual boundaries of the life cycles of the biorefineries including the stages of transportation, cultivation (when required), processing, and waste disposal.

The most recent data are used for this assessment and it is con- sidered that the data will be viable for a period of 10 years based on the available technologies for biomass processing, biogas use, and waste sludge disposal. For biogas production from macroal- gae, credit is allocated to the CO2 absorbed during macroalgae cul- tivation. For biogas production from cattle manure, the environmental credit is allocated to the reuse of the manure, which would have otherwise been washed away to surrounding water bodies. Environmental credit is also allocated to the heat co-product obtained from the two biorefineries when the biogas is used for electricity production because this heat can be reused instead of depending on thermal energy from a natural gas-fired or diesel-fired boiler. The macroalgae biorefinery is equipped with an open pond and fed with saline water and sludge from a large- scale municipal wastewater treatment plant (Al Mafraq plant, Abu Dhabi) that supplies the required nutrients. The biorefinery is modeled to be located at a distance of 100 m from the wastewater plant.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis: (1) the internal walls of the open pond are made of ethylene propylenediene monomer (EPDM) rubber to prevent leaching of nutrients and salts to the ground; (2) initial algal biomass is first incubated in a protoplast incubator before it is fed to the pond. The protoplasts of Enteromorpha (Ulva) prolifera algae, a common green alga with tubular fronds and flattened branches, are obtained from the incubator; (3) macroalgal growth is ensured by CO2 from the exhaust from the petroleum refinery around the plant. CO2 from industrial sources such as petroleum refineries can increase macroalgal growth rate by a factor of 1.21.8; (4) the daily growth rate of macroalgae is estimated from the correlation or dependence of macroalgal growth on temperature variations, according to Eq. (1).

where DGR is daily growth rate of macrolgae and T is ambient tem- perature in Since macroalgal growth is also dependent on nutri- ent availability and salinity, the composition of the inlet sludge is considered to be constant; (5) the volume of sludge input required for the functional unit is 8.45 m³, i.e. 202 kg with 23% solid matter. In addition, the concentration of the saline water is kept at the aver- age salinity of Gulf water, i.e. 35,000 ppm. The algal biomass is then harvested and sent to a two-stage anaerobic digester for conversion

to biogas; (6) the total length of pipeline (galvanized steel pipes) considered for biogas processing is 109.2 km. A stainless steel dis- charge decanter (centrifuge) is used to dewater the harvested algae slurry before anaerobic digestion. The slurry is fed to the centrifuges basket, from where the solid phase goes to the inner wall under centrifugal force; (7) in the first stage of the two-stage digester, the feed is acidified at 38 for hydrolysis. After hydrolysis, the solid part is recycled to the first stage while the liquid part (effluent) is sent to the second stage. The second stage of digestion involves the use of a rotor to ensure that the effluent is exposed to a high concentration of methanogenic bacteria and conversion to biogas is optimized. 32.9 kg of free-flowing light brown odorless methano- genic bacteria powder is required to produce the functional unit; (8) the digestate from the digester is separated by a screw press into a solid part which is dried and landfilled, and a liquid part which is reused in the open pond for macroalgae cultivation. The screw press is a stainless steel automatic-running screw press used to dewater the sludge by gravity and the inner pressure is imparted by a grad- ually narrowed-pitch distance; (9) the biogas is stored in a spherical dual membrane tank made from steel fabric with a volume of 45.5 m³ and the location of biogas conversion to electricity is con- sidered to be 5 km away from the storage tank. The processing of macroalgae to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 2.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis of the cattle manure biorefinery: (1) the biorefinery is also con- sidered to be located at a distance of 100 m from Al Mafraq wastewater treatment plant; (2) the plant is equipped with a man- ure collection tank (a glass-lined steel tank) from where the cattle manure is sent to a homogenization tank for mixing; (3) the homogenization tank is equipped with a propeller-type mixer to facilitate the blending of the waste stream. The uniformly mixed waste is passed into an anaerobic digester where biogas is pro- duced; (4) in the anaerobic digester, the blended stream is con- verted to biogas by anaerobic bacteria (volatile suspended solids). The digester works optimally at neutral pH and mesophilic temperature of around 35 C. The carbon-nitrogen ratio of the feed for optimal biogas production should be in the standard range of 20:130:1 (Wang et al., 2012). In this study, the carbon-nitrogen ratio of 25:1 is used; (5) the digestate is then sent to a screw press where a liquid fraction is obtained and recycled back to the anaer- obic digester; (6) the solid part is dried and landfilled. The process- ing of cattle manure to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 3.
Fig. 2. Schematic of the processing of macroalgae to biogas.
Fig. 3. Schematic of the processing of cattle manure to biogas.

where DGR is daily growth rate of macrolgae and T is ambient tem- perature in Since macroalgal growth is also dependent on nutri- ent availability and salinity, the composition of the inlet sludge is considered to be constant; (5) the volume of sludge input required for the functional unit is 8.45 m³, i.e. 202 kg with 23% solid matter. In addition, the concentration of the saline water is kept at the aver- age salinity of Gulf water, i.e. 35,000 ppm. The algal biomass is then harvested and sent to a two-stage anaerobic digester for conversion

to biogas; (6) the total length of pipeline (galvanized steel pipes) considered for biogas processing is 109.2 km. A stainless steel dis- charge decanter (centrifuge) is used to dewater the harvested algae slurry before anaerobic digestion. The slurry is fed to the centrifuges basket, from where the solid phase goes to the inner wall under centrifugal force; (7) in the first stage of the two-stage digester, the feed is acidified at 38 for hydrolysis. After hydrolysis, the solid part is recycled to the first stage while the liquid part (effluent) is sent to the second stage. The second stage of digestion involves the use of a rotor to ensure that the effluent is exposed to a high concentration of methanogenic bacteria and conversion to biogas is optimized. 32.9 kg of free-flowing light brown odorless methano- genic bacteria powder is required to produce the functional unit; (8) the digestate from the digester is separated by a screw press into a solid part which is dried and landfilled, and a liquid part which is reused in the open pond for macroalgae cultivation. The screw press is a stainless steel automatic-running screw press used to dewater the sludge by gravity and the inner pressure is imparted by a grad- ually narrowed-pitch distance; (9) the biogas is stored in a spherical dual membrane tank made from steel fabric with a volume of 45.5 m³ and the location of biogas conversion to electricity is con- sidered to be 5 km away from the storage tank. The processing of macroalgae to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 2.

The following considerations are made in the functional analy- sis of the cattle manure biorefinery: (1) the biorefinery is also con- sidered to be located at a distance of 100 m from Al Mafraq wastewater treatment plant; (2) the plant is equipped with a man- ure collection tank (a glass-lined steel tank) from where the cattle manure is sent to a homogenization tank for mixing; (3) the homogenization tank is equipped with a propeller-type mixer to facilitate the blending of the waste stream. The uniformly mixed waste is passed into an anaerobic digester where biogas is pro- duced; (4) in the anaerobic digester, the blended stream is con- verted to biogas by anaerobic bacteria (volatile suspended solids). The digester works optimally at neutral pH and mesophilic temperature of around 35 C. The carbon-nitrogen ratio of the feed for optimal biogas production should be in the standard range of 20:130:1 (Wang et al., 2012). In this study, the carbon-nitrogen ratio of 25:1 is used; (5) the digestate is then sent to a screw press where a liquid fraction is obtained and recycled back to the anaer- obic digester; (6) the solid part is dried and landfilled. The process- ing of cattle manure to biogas is illustrated in Fig. 3.
Fig. 2. Schematic of the processing of macroalgae to biogas.
Fig. 3. Schematic of the processing of cattle manure to biogas.

The input and output data for the processes in the two biore- fineries are obtained from open sources. They are then normalized to the functional unit. These inventory data are presented as mate- rial, energy and transportation requirements responsible for the emissions to the environment (land, water and air). The environ- mental loads involved in the manufacture of the capital goods are excluded. The inputs and outputs that are directly associated with the production and use of biogas, and disposal of the waste sludge are identified and quantified. The environmental impact assessment is based on Eco-indicator 99 hierarchist method. The hierarchist method includes all substances that are deemed car- cinogenic based on international scientific consensus. The other

two possible methods that can be employed in Eco-indicator 99 LCA approach are the individualistic and egalitarian method. The hierarchist method is used in this impact assessment because the impacts are analyzed based on scientifically proven data. However, this method is further compared with the individualistic and legal- itarian views in the sensitivity analysis to see the influence of these views on the impacts.

In the Eco-indicator 99 approach, there are 11 impact (or mid- point) categories and 3 damage (or endpoint) categories. In this study, the impacts are assessed with respect to the impact cate- gories because the damage categories are also the reflection of the impact categories. The impact categories include the impacts due to carcinogens, respiratory organics, respiratory inorganics, cli- mate change, radiation, ozone layer, ecotoxicity, acidification and

eutrophication, land use, minerals, and fossil fuels. The scores in the impact categories are further normalized based on the average impact on one person in one year, i.e. 1 point Pt) is the average yearly environmental impacts contributed by one person in Eur- ope. This is known as the single score Eco-indicator point. A single impact score that is less than 1 Pt means that the activity or pro- cess contributes less than the average environmental load per cap- ita in Europe. The scale is normally represented in mPt, which is one-thousandth of the yearly environmental impact of an average European inhabitant. Europe is the built-in Eco-indicator reference for 1 Pt in Simapro 7.3.3. The inventory data used for the impact assessment of the two biorefineries is provided in Table 1.

Three scenarios are assessed and compared. Scenario 1 is the base scenario, which involves the use of the biogas for electricity production through CO2 and H2S removal by water scrubbing fol- lowed by combustion to generate steam that will run a turbine. In scenario 2, the application is changed from electricity produc- tion to automobile transportation. For this scenario, the following considerations are made: (1) the biogas is cleaned and then com- pressed into automobile fuel through the removal of H2S from the biogas by biological oxidation due to its highly corrosive nat- ure. The removal of H2S takes place in a biological desulphurization unit in which a limited quantity of air is added to the biogas in the presence of sulfur oxidizing bacteria that oxidizes H2S into elemen- tal sulfur; (2) compression via a compressor and storage in pres- surized cylinder to ensure that the gas can be easily transported to places where it will be utilized as automobile fuel. In Scenarios 1 and 2, all input energy requirements are considered to be sourced from the natural gas-powered grid. In Scenario 3, however, the energy obtained from the biogas is considered to be recycled and reused in the systems.

eutrophication, land use, minerals, and fossil fuels. The scores in the impact categories are further normalized based on the average impact on one person in one year, i.e. 1 point Pt) is the average yearly environmental impacts contributed by one person in Eur- ope. This is known as the single score Eco-indicator point. A single impact score that is less than 1 Pt means that the activity or pro- cess contributes less than the average environmental load per cap- ita in Europe. The scale is normally represented in mPt, which is one-thousandth of the yearly environmental impact of an average European inhabitant. Europe is the built-in Eco-indicator reference for 1 Pt in Simapro 7.3.3. The inventory data used for the impact assessment of the two biorefineries is provided in Table 1.

Three scenarios are assessed and compared. Scenario 1 is the base scenario, which involves the use of the biogas for electricity production through CO2 and H2S removal by water scrubbing fol- lowed by combustion to generate steam that will run a turbine. In scenario 2, the application is changed from electricity produc- tion to automobile transportation. For this scenario, the following considerations are made: (1) the biogas is cleaned and then com- pressed into automobile fuel through the removal of H2S from the biogas by biological oxidation due to its highly corrosive nat- ure. The removal of H2S takes place in a biological desulphurization unit in which a limited quantity of air is added to the biogas in the presence of sulfur oxidizing bacteria that oxidizes H2S into elemen- tal sulfur; (2) compression via a compressor and storage in pres- surized cylinder to ensure that the gas can be easily transported to places where it will be utilized as automobile fuel. In Scenarios 1 and 2, all input energy requirements are considered to be sourced from the natural gas-powered grid. In Scenario 3, however, the energy obtained from the biogas is considered to be recycled and reused in the systems.

30: For the base scenario, the life cycle (single score total impact) of biogas production from macroalgae is 2112 mPt which is slightly higher than 2017 mPt obtained for biogas production from cattle manure. These impact points are obtained from the inventory data supplied as inputs to Simapro 7.3.3 for both biorefineries. The energy and material usage and environmental releases by the inventories are converted to environmental impact points using the in-built characterization factor for each inventory in Simapro 7.3.3. These are relatively low impact points because the process- ing of biomass to biogas is known to have low environmental impact. This is because the gases produced are trapped, rather than released to the environment. It has been reported recently that the single score impact points of gasoline and kerosene production via petroleum refining are 11,100 mPt and 4830 mPt for every m³ of each fuel (Alanbari et al., 2016). These are values that are much higher than the values obtained for the macroalgae and cattle man-
Impact points contributed by the midpoint impact categories for the base scenario.

Impact Category|Cattle Manure (mPt)|Macroalgae (mPt) Fossil fuels|1131.89|1183.61 Resp. inorganics|567.98|602.21 Acidification/Eutrophication|88.42|95.39 Climate change|73.46|75.38 Land use|63.84|66.19 Minerals|39.90|39.76 Carcinogens|25.40|24.32 Ecotoxicity|24.48|23.76 Radiation|0.78|0.77 Resp. organics|0.67|0.70 Ozone Layer|0.03|0.03 Total|2016.85|2112.12

ure biorefineries. Meanwhile, despite the environmental credit of CO2 sequestration allocated to the macroalgae biorefinery, the total impact of the life cycle of this biorefinery is slightly higher than that of the cattle manure biorefinery. The slight difference is mainly due to the transportation energy requirement and carbon gases released during the transportation of the capital goods and process materials. Table 2 shows the single score point contributed by each impact for the base scenario of both biorefineries, in decreasing order of magnitude.

The highest impact is from fossil fuel used for grid energy pro- duction and transportation. The impact contributed by anaerobic decomposition is minimal because the major emissions from this phase are collected in the form of biogas, rather than being released to the environment. The impact from respiratory inorgan- ics is also high due to similar reasons. Respiratory inorganics are the particulate matter produced from the combustion of fossil fuels. This particulate matter includes sulfate and nitrate aerosols that cause respiratory difficulties. The potential of nutrient release from the biorefineries and eventual accumulation in the ground- water and surrounding open water bodies also raises concern for eutrophication, in the case of process failure.

Following these two aforementioned impact categories in terms of decreasing order of impact points are acidification/eutrophica- tion, climate change and land use for both processes. The impacts of both biorefineries are in the following order: Fossil fuel > Respi- ratory inorganics > Acidification and eutrophication > Climate change > Land use > Minerals > Carcinogens > Ecotoxicity > Radia- tion > Respiratory organics > Ozone layer depletion. Acidification and eutrophication are closely related to the particulate matter of sulfate and nitrate from fuel combustion. Sulfate releases toxic SO2 gas which can combine with water bodies in the environment to form sulfuric acid. Acidification can also arise from the CO2 released during the burning of fossil fuels, as ocean pH levels can be decreased by CO2 absorption. Eutrophication, on the other hand, arises from excess nutrient in a water body which can be facilitated by dissolved nitrate. Both biorefineries have impacts related to acidification and eutrophication because the fossil fuels used for transportation and grid electricity production release acidic SO2 and CO2, and eutrophic nitrate during their high-temperature com- bustion. In addition, these gases contribute to climate change. Although macroalgae grow under halophilic conditions and the cattle manure is sourced from the farms, land use still constitutes a relatively considerable impact. This impact evidently arises from the changes to the existing land use, including changes to land availability for commercial processes, tourism, and cultivation of desert crops such as date seeds in Abu Dhabi. The land use change can be direct or indirect; direct change results in direct CO2 emis- sion due to a change from one land use to another while indirect land use occurs when a land that is used previously to grow food crops is now used for biogas production such that the new land for food production results in CO2 emissions (Wicke et al., 2012). In this study, only direct land use change is considered, as no pre- vious cultivation of food on the land is considered.

Although the single score impact of the cattle manure biorefin- ery is lower than that of the macroalgae biorefinery, the energy and water consumed during the biorefining favor macroalgae, as shown in Table 1. Lower consumption of energy and water makes the macroalgae biorefinery to be more eco-friendly in the process- ing and disposal stages. The ratio of the impact points contributed by the macroalgae and cattle manure biorefineries in the process- ing stage is 49:51. In addition, the ratio is 40:60 during the sludge disposal stage because the anaerobic digestion of the cattle manure produces more waste. However, the impact point contributed by transportation is higher for the macroalgae biorefinery because macroalgae needs to be cultivated and the materials required for macroalgae cultivation needs to be transported to the pond site.
Fig. 4. Single score impact points for the three scenarios. CM = cattle manure; M = macroalgae; (1) = base scenario; (2) = scenario 2; (3) = scenario 3. The base scenario is scenario 1.

This makes the animal manure biorefinery to pose lower environ- mental stress when the entire life cycles of the two biorefineries are considered, as the impact of the transportation of materials required for pond construction and macroalgae cultivation out- weighs the environmental gains attributed to the CO2 sequestra- tion by macroalgae.

Scenarios 2 and 3 are assessed and compared with the base sce- nario. Since the major impacts are from fossil fuels and respiratory inorganics, the response of the environmental impacts to a change in transportation fuel is studied in Scenario 2. This scenario involves the recycling of the biogas produced and its use for the land transportation of the inputs (raw materials and capital goods) sourced locally, instead of depending on refined fossil fuels. In Sce- nario 1, all inputs are considered to be transported by fossil fuel- powered transportation systems. Scenario 2 gives lower single score impacts than Scenario 1 for both biorefineries, but the single score impact of the life cycle of the cattle manure biorefinery is still lower than that of macroalgae biorefinery (Fig. 4). There is a more significant reduction in impacts due to fossil fuels, respiratory inor- ganics and acidification/eutrophication resulting from air and water transportation in Scenario 2 because the biogas produced was used for land transportation. Conceptual stages such as pro- cessing and disposal now contribute more significantly to the total environmental impact than the transportation stage in Scenario 2, but the total single score impacts for both biorefineries are lower than those obtained for scenario 1. The single score impact of the cattle manure biorefinery reduces by 11% (to 1795 mPt) and that of macroalgae reduces by 10.5% (to 1890 mPt) in Scenario 2, rela- tive to Scenario 1, as a result of the change in the biogas use. Reduction in the use of fossil fuels for transportation is the most significant contributor to the impact reduction in Scenario 2. The single score impacts become further reduced in Scenario 3 when the source of energy to run the two biorefineries is changed from the natural gas-powered grid electricity to the biogas energy recycled from the biorefineries. In Scenarios 1 and 2, electricity from the grid is considered as the source of energy. The single score impact of the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries reduce by 51.3% (to 983 mPt) and 58.6% (to 875 mPt), respectively, in Sce- nario 3, relative to Scenario 1. This huge reduction is as a result of the replacement of the energy used in the biorefineries, as grid- connected energy is responsible for more than half of the total impacts in Scenario 1. Meanwhile, in Scenario 3, the macroalgae biorefinery has less total impact point than the cattle manure

biorefinery because it requires less energy for biogas production, i.e. 54173.8 MJ, as shown in Table 1. So, the environmental impact of the macroalgae biorefinery can be significantly reduced by recy- cling the bioenergy produced back to the process.

The life cycle impacts of the two biorefineries are also analyzed and compared in terms of the specific emissions released through the combustion of fossil fuels for transportation and grid electricity production. These emissions include nitrogen oxides (NO2 and N20), SO2, and particulate aerosols <2.5 um. For the cattle manure biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission are reduced by 4% and 65% in Scenarios 2 and 3, respectively, with respect to Scenario 1 (Fig. 5A). For the macroalgae biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission reduced by 3.6% and 71%, respectively in Scenarios 2 and 3 (Fig. 5B). Scenario 3 results in a more substantial decrease in nitrogen oxides emission. Also, Scenario 3 reduced the impact due to SO2 emission by 85% and 88% for the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries, respec- tively, as compared to the base scenario.

Unlike the hierarchist method, the individualistic method con- siders all substances that are carcinogenic based on both scientific and non-scientific (historical) data while the egalitarian method considers all substances that can possibly be carcinogenic, regard- less of whether the proofs are available or not (Laleman et al., 2011; Onn and Yusoff, 2010). The Individualistic view agrees with the Hierarchist view but with higher impact points for both biore- fineries (Fig. 5C). However, the egalitarian view results in less total impact points because it allocates more weighting to the environ- mental credit allocated to the CO2 sequestration and reuse of cattle manure. In addition, this view favors macroalgae, as lower total impact point is obtained for the macroalgae biorefinery, because it places more emphasis on the CO2 sequestration of macroalgae.

The impact points of all midpoint and endpoint categories for all scenarios are provided in Appendix A. The endpoint categories are those relating to the environmental impacts at the end of the cause-effect chain, including human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. The most significant inputs (materials, energy and emissions) influencing the impact points of the best case scenario, and the computer screenshot of the Simapro software are also pro- vided in Appendix A.

Meanwhile, there are drivers and barriers towards the imple- mentation of these biorefineries. Policy and decision makers have a major role to play in ensuring that the drivers are properly iden- tified and the barriers are adequately addressed. The practicality of

Fig. 5. Emissions released from the life cycles of the (A) cattle manure and (B) macroalgae biorefineries. (C) Impact points based on Hierarchist (Eco99-H), Egalitarian (Eco99- E), and Individualistic (Eco99-I) views for cattle manure (CM) and macroalgae (M) biorefineries.

this comparative assessment also depends on the readiness of the existing infrastructure and cost of biogas production. As at 2012, the total energy consumption in the UAE per capita was 7.5 toe one of the highest in the world. Of the total energy consumed, only 0.13% was contributed by renewable energy. Consequently, the CO2 emission from energy generation in the UAE was 40.31 ton per capita the 6th largest in the world (Guardian, 2011; The World Bank, 2015). Most of the power plants in the UAE are run using natural gas. In addition, natural gas is the fuel for most vehi- cles used for public transport. Therefore, the main driver for the substitution of natural gas with biogas in the UAE is climate change and fossil fuel depletion. However, the high cost of biogas produc- tion in the UAE, as compared to natural gas is a barrier (Belasri et al., 2016). Apart from capital and material transportation costs, the hydrolysis step of the processing stage also contributes signif- icantly to the cost (Yadvika et al., 2004).

There are no reported values for the cost of biogas production from large-scale macroalgae or cattle manure biorefineries in the UAE, as such plants are not available currently. However, the typ- ical levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) from biogas can reach up to USD 0.25/kWh, depending on whether financial support is avail- able or not (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). This cost is not competitive, when it is compared to the cost of other energy sources in the country. The estimated cost of nuclear power in the UAE is USD 0.11/kWh while the cost of natu- ral gas power is USD 0.07/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016) The world record for the cost of solar PV power was established in the UAE in 2016 at USD 0.024/kWh (Binsal Abdul Kader, 2017). The best wind projects are delivering

electricity at USD 0.05/kWh without financial support (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). Coal is expected to generate electric power at USD 0.045/kWh in Dubai once the planned coal plant is operational in 2020 (Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA), 2017), while the cost of electric power production from diesel is USD 0.120.16/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016). Clearly, other available energy sources are cheaper than biogas due to their high conversion efficiencies.

Externalities resulting from the use of natural gas and other fos- sil fuels are also excluded from their current market prices, thereby giving them a competitive economic edge over biogas. Another barrier is the lack of skills and long-term experience. There are very few biorefineries in the country and most of them were commis- sioned recently. In addition, the lack of necessary infrastructure is a critical barrier. To start a full-scale biorefinery in the country, most of the equipment required for the construction would be imported from other countries. As it can be seen from Table 1, cap- ital goods are mainly transported from other countries.

To overcome this barrier, political, social and technological interventions are inevitable. On the political side, the UAE plans to increase the percentage of renewable energy generated through nuclear, solar and biomass energy to 7% by 2020. The UAE also plans to generate 24% of its electricity from clean energy sources by 2021, according to the agreement signed by the country at the COP21 United Nations Climate Change Conference in December 2015 (Embassy of the United Arab Emirates, 2016). The economic hub of the country, i.e. Dubai emirate, hopes to increase the per- centage of renewable energy generated in the emirate to 75% by

2050 (Emirates News Agency, 2016). For biogas production, there are plans to introduce a feed-in-tariff that offers a fixed attractive price in order to create a market for biogas and recover the cost of production. Biogas production quotas would be assigned to pro- ducers and inventors to ensure that the market share required is achieved, as part of the broader renewable energy policy. Another political intervention is the practice of contract biddings or conces- sion for bioenergy projects. Direct subsidies in form of tax rebates have been introduced to lessen the risks associated with the high initial (capital) cost of renewable energy plants (Abdmouleh et al., 2015; Mezher et al., 2012).

The recent downtrend in the prices of fossil-based fuels globally has also encouraged political interventions in the implementation of biogas projects. The declining crude oil prices have led to falling state revenues and triggered a series of energy price reforms recently. With crude oil priced at less than USD 50/bbl currently, the wide price gap between crude oil and renewables has been lessened. As a result of this, the national oil company (TAQA) has initiated the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) of UAEs first waste-to-energy plant that is expected to produce 100 MW of electric power, which is enough to power 20,000 houses (Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (TAQA), 2017, Jamil et al., 2016).

On the social side, more awareness campaigns are required to encourage energy stakeholders to utilize macroalgae and cattle manure for large-scale biogas production. The marginal cost at lar- ger scales might eventually bring down the cost of biogas produc- tion. On the technological side, the critical infrastructure needed to ensure commercial deployment of biogas would have to be pro- vided. Scientific research on enhanced macroalgal conversion tech- nologies involving molecular bioengineering (Jung et al., 2013), supercritical water gasification (Patel et al., 2016), and the utiliza- tion of atmospheric CO2 captured in geological reservoirs for algal growth (Moreira and Pires, 2016) should be intensified.

These are relatively low impact points because the process- ing of biomass to biogas is known to have low environmental impact.
