These are values that are much higher than the values obtained for the macroalgae and cattle man-
Impact points contributed by the midpoint impact categories for the base scenario.

Impact Category|Cattle Manure (mPt)|Macroalgae (mPt) Fossil fuels|1131.89|1183.61 Resp. inorganics|567.98|602.21 Acidification/Eutrophication|88.42|95.39 Climate change|73.46|75.38 Land use|63.84|66.19 Minerals|39.90|39.76 Carcinogens|25.40|24.32 Ecotoxicity|24.48|23.76 Radiation|0.78|0.77 Resp. organics|0.67|0.70 Ozone Layer|0.03|0.03 Total|2016.85|2112.12

ure biorefineries. Meanwhile, despite the environmental credit of CO2 sequestration allocated to the macroalgae biorefinery, the total impact of the life cycle of this biorefinery is slightly higher than that of the cattle manure biorefinery. The slight difference is mainly due to the transportation energy requirement and carbon gases released during the transportation of the capital goods and process materials. Table 2 shows the single score point contributed by each impact for the base scenario of both biorefineries, in decreasing order of magnitude.

The highest impact is from fossil fuel used for grid energy pro- duction and transportation. The impact contributed by anaerobic decomposition is minimal because the major emissions from this phase are collected in the form of biogas, rather than being released to the environment. The impact from respiratory inorgan- ics is also high due to similar reasons. Respiratory inorganics are the particulate matter produced from the combustion of fossil fuels. This particulate matter includes sulfate and nitrate aerosols that cause respiratory difficulties. The potential of nutrient release from the biorefineries and eventual accumulation in the ground- water and surrounding open water bodies also raises concern for eutrophication, in the case of process failure.

Following these two aforementioned impact categories in terms of decreasing order of impact points are acidification/eutrophica- tion, climate change and land use for both processes. The impacts of both biorefineries are in the following order: Fossil fuel > Respi- ratory inorganics > Acidification and eutrophication > Climate change > Land use > Minerals > Carcinogens > Ecotoxicity > Radia- tion > Respiratory organics > Ozone layer depletion. Acidification and eutrophication are closely related to the particulate matter of sulfate and nitrate from fuel combustion. Sulfate releases toxic SO2 gas which can combine with water bodies in the environment to form sulfuric acid. Acidification can also arise from the CO2 released during the burning of fossil fuels, as ocean pH levels can be decreased by CO2 absorption. Eutrophication, on the other hand, arises from excess nutrient in a water body which can be facilitated by dissolved nitrate. Both biorefineries have impacts related to acidification and eutrophication because the fossil fuels used for transportation and grid electricity production release acidic SO2 and CO2, and eutrophic nitrate during their high-temperature com- bustion. In addition, these gases contribute to climate change. Although macroalgae grow under halophilic conditions and the cattle manure is sourced from the farms, land use still constitutes a relatively considerable impact. This impact evidently arises from the changes to the existing land use, including changes to land availability for commercial processes, tourism, and cultivation of desert crops such as date seeds in Abu Dhabi. The land use change can be direct or indirect; direct change results in direct CO2 emis- sion due to a change from one land use to another while indirect land use occurs when a land that is used previously to grow food crops is now used for biogas production such that the new land for food production results in CO2 emissions (Wicke et al., 2012). In this study, only direct land use change is considered, as no pre- vious cultivation of food on the land is considered.

Although the single score impact of the cattle manure biorefin- ery is lower than that of the macroalgae biorefinery, the energy and water consumed during the biorefining favor macroalgae, as shown in Table 1. Lower consumption of energy and water makes the macroalgae biorefinery to be more eco-friendly in the process- ing and disposal stages. The ratio of the impact points contributed by the macroalgae and cattle manure biorefineries in the process- ing stage is 49:51. In addition, the ratio is 40:60 during the sludge disposal stage because the anaerobic digestion of the cattle manure produces more waste. However, the impact point contributed by transportation is higher for the macroalgae biorefinery because macroalgae needs to be cultivated and the materials required for macroalgae cultivation needs to be transported to the pond site.
Fig. 4. Single score impact points for the three scenarios. CM = cattle manure; M = macroalgae; (1) = base scenario; (2) = scenario 2; (3) = scenario 3. The base scenario is scenario 1.

This makes the animal manure biorefinery to pose lower environ- mental stress when the entire life cycles of the two biorefineries are considered, as the impact of the transportation of materials required for pond construction and macroalgae cultivation out- weighs the environmental gains attributed to the CO2 sequestra- tion by macroalgae.

Scenarios 2 and 3 are assessed and compared with the base sce- nario. Since the major impacts are from fossil fuels and respiratory inorganics, the response of the environmental impacts to a change in transportation fuel is studied in Scenario 2. This scenario involves the recycling of the biogas produced and its use for the land transportation of the inputs (raw materials and capital goods) sourced locally, instead of depending on refined fossil fuels. In Sce- nario 1, all inputs are considered to be transported by fossil fuel- powered transportation systems. Scenario 2 gives lower single score impacts than Scenario 1 for both biorefineries, but the single score impact of the life cycle of the cattle manure biorefinery is still lower than that of macroalgae biorefinery (Fig. 4). There is a more significant reduction in impacts due to fossil fuels, respiratory inor- ganics and acidification/eutrophication resulting from air and water transportation in Scenario 2 because the biogas produced was used for land transportation. Conceptual stages such as pro- cessing and disposal now contribute more significantly to the total environmental impact than the transportation stage in Scenario 2, but the total single score impacts for both biorefineries are lower than those obtained for scenario 1. The single score impact of the cattle manure biorefinery reduces by 11% (to 1795 mPt) and that of macroalgae reduces by 10.5% (to 1890 mPt) in Scenario 2, rela- tive to Scenario 1, as a result of the change in the biogas use. Reduction in the use of fossil fuels for transportation is the most significant contributor to the impact reduction in Scenario 2. The single score impacts become further reduced in Scenario 3 when the source of energy to run the two biorefineries is changed from the natural gas-powered grid electricity to the biogas energy recycled from the biorefineries. In Scenarios 1 and 2, electricity from the grid is considered as the source of energy. The single score impact of the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries reduce by 51.3% (to 983 mPt) and 58.6% (to 875 mPt), respectively, in Sce- nario 3, relative to Scenario 1. This huge reduction is as a result of the replacement of the energy used in the biorefineries, as grid- connected energy is responsible for more than half of the total impacts in Scenario 1. Meanwhile, in Scenario 3, the macroalgae biorefinery has less total impact point than the cattle manure

biorefinery because it requires less energy for biogas production, i.e. 54173.8 MJ, as shown in Table 1. So, the environmental impact of the macroalgae biorefinery can be significantly reduced by recy- cling the bioenergy produced back to the process.

The life cycle impacts of the two biorefineries are also analyzed and compared in terms of the specific emissions released through the combustion of fossil fuels for transportation and grid electricity production. These emissions include nitrogen oxides (NO2 and N20), SO2, and particulate aerosols <2.5 um. For the cattle manure biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission are reduced by 4% and 65% in Scenarios 2 and 3, respectively, with respect to Scenario 1 (Fig. 5A). For the macroalgae biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission reduced by 3.6% and 71%, respectively in Scenarios 2 and 3 (Fig. 5B). Scenario 3 results in a more substantial decrease in nitrogen oxides emission. Also, Scenario 3 reduced the impact due to SO2 emission by 85% and 88% for the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries, respec- tively, as compared to the base scenario.

Unlike the hierarchist method, the individualistic method con- siders all substances that are carcinogenic based on both scientific and non-scientific (historical) data while the egalitarian method considers all substances that can possibly be carcinogenic, regard- less of whether the proofs are available or not (Laleman et al., 2011; Onn and Yusoff, 2010). The Individualistic view agrees with the Hierarchist view but with higher impact points for both biore- fineries (Fig. 5C). However, the egalitarian view results in less total impact points because it allocates more weighting to the environ- mental credit allocated to the CO2 sequestration and reuse of cattle manure. In addition, this view favors macroalgae, as lower total impact point is obtained for the macroalgae biorefinery, because it places more emphasis on the CO2 sequestration of macroalgae.

The impact points of all midpoint and endpoint categories for all scenarios are provided in Appendix A. The endpoint categories are those relating to the environmental impacts at the end of the cause-effect chain, including human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. The most significant inputs (materials, energy and emissions) influencing the impact points of the best case scenario, and the computer screenshot of the Simapro software are also pro- vided in Appendix A.

Meanwhile, there are drivers and barriers towards the imple- mentation of these biorefineries. Policy and decision makers have a major role to play in ensuring that the drivers are properly iden- tified and the barriers are adequately addressed. The practicality of

this comparative assessment also depends on the readiness of the existing infrastructure and cost of biogas production. As at 2012, the total energy consumption in the UAE per capita was 7.5 toe one of the highest in the world. Of the total energy consumed, only 0.13% was contributed by renewable energy. Consequently, the CO2 emission from energy generation in the UAE was 40.31 ton per capita the 6th largest in the world (Guardian, 2011; The World Bank, 2015). Most of the power plants in the UAE are run using natural gas. In addition, natural gas is the fuel for most vehi- cles used for public transport. Therefore, the main driver for the substitution of natural gas with biogas in the UAE is climate change and fossil fuel depletion. However, the high cost of biogas produc- tion in the UAE, as compared to natural gas is a barrier (Belasri et al., 2016). Apart from capital and material transportation costs, the hydrolysis step of the processing stage also contributes signif- icantly to the cost (Yadvika et al., 2004).

There are no reported values for the cost of biogas production from large-scale macroalgae or cattle manure biorefineries in the UAE, as such plants are not available currently. However, the typ- ical levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) from biogas can reach up to USD 0.25/kWh, depending on whether financial support is avail- able or not (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). This cost is not competitive, when it is compared to the cost of other energy sources in the country. The estimated cost of nuclear power in the UAE is USD 0.11/kWh while the cost of natu- ral gas power is USD 0.07/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016) The world record for the cost of solar PV power was established in the UAE in 2016 at USD 0.024/kWh (Binsal Abdul Kader, 2017). The best wind projects are delivering

electricity at USD 0.05/kWh without financial support (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). Coal is expected to generate electric power at USD 0.045/kWh in Dubai once the planned coal plant is operational in 2020 (Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA), 2017), while the cost of electric power production from diesel is USD 0.120.16/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016). However, the typ- ical levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) from biogas can reach up to USD 0.25/kWh, depending on whether financial support is avail- able or not (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). This cost is not competitive, when it is compared to the cost of other energy sources in the country. The estimated cost of nuclear power in the UAE is USD 0.11/kWh while the cost of natu- ral gas power is USD 0.07/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016) The world record for the cost of solar PV power was established in the UAE in 2016 at USD 0.024/kWh (Binsal Abdul Kader, 2017). The best wind projects are delivering

electricity at USD 0.05/kWh without financial support (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). Coal is expected to generate electric power at USD 0.045/kWh in Dubai once the planned coal plant is operational in 2020 (Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA), 2017), while the cost of electric power production from diesel is USD 0.120.16/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016). Clearly, other available energy sources are cheaper than biogas due to their high conversion efficiencies.

Externalities resulting from the use of natural gas and other fos- sil fuels are also excluded from their current market prices, thereby giving them a competitive economic edge over biogas. Another barrier is the lack of skills and long-term experience. There are very few biorefineries in the country and most of them were commis- sioned recently. In addition, the lack of necessary infrastructure is a critical barrier. To start a full-scale biorefinery in the country, most of the equipment required for the construction would be imported from other countries. As it can be seen from Table 1, cap- ital goods are mainly transported from other countries.

To overcome this barrier, political, social and technological interventions are inevitable. On the political side, the UAE plans to increase the percentage of renewable energy generated through nuclear, solar and biomass energy to 7% by 2020. The UAE also plans to generate 24% of its electricity from clean energy sources by 2021, according to the agreement signed by the country at the COP21 United Nations Climate Change Conference in December 2015 (Embassy of the United Arab Emirates, 2016). The economic hub of the country, i.e. Dubai emirate, hopes to increase the per- centage of renewable energy generated in the emirate to 75% by

2050 (Emirates News Agency, 2016). For biogas production, there are plans to introduce a feed-in-tariff that offers a fixed attractive price in order to create a market for biogas and recover the cost of production. Biogas production quotas would be assigned to pro- ducers and inventors to ensure that the market share required is achieved, as part of the broader renewable energy policy. Another political intervention is the practice of contract biddings or conces- sion for bioenergy projects. Direct subsidies in form of tax rebates have been introduced to lessen the risks associated with the high initial (capital) cost of renewable energy plants (Abdmouleh et al., 2015; Mezher et al., 2012).

The recent downtrend in the prices of fossil-based fuels globally has also encouraged political interventions in the implementation of biogas projects. The declining crude oil prices have led to falling state revenues and triggered a series of energy price reforms recently. With crude oil priced at less than USD 50/bbl currently, the wide price gap between crude oil and renewables has been lessened. As a result of this, the national oil company (TAQA) has initiated the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) of UAEs first waste-to-energy plant that is expected to produce 100 MW of electric power, which is enough to power 20,000 houses (Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (TAQA), 2017, Jamil et al., 2016).

On the social side, more awareness campaigns are required to encourage energy stakeholders to utilize macroalgae and cattle manure for large-scale biogas production. The marginal cost at lar- ger scales might eventually bring down the cost of biogas produc- tion. On the technological side, the critical infrastructure needed to ensure commercial deployment of biogas would have to be pro- vided. Scientific research on enhanced macroalgal conversion tech- nologies involving molecular bioengineering (Jung et al., 2013), supercritical water gasification (Patel et al., 2016), and the utiliza- tion of atmospheric CO2 captured in geological reservoirs for algal growth (Moreira and Pires, 2016) should be intensified.

These are relatively low impact points because the process- ing of biomass to biogas is known to have low environmental impact. This is because the gases produced are trapped, rather than released to the environment. It has been reported recently that the single score impact points of gasoline and kerosene production via petroleum refining are 11,100 mPt and 4830 mPt for every m³ of each fuel (Alanbari et al., 2016). These are values that are much higher than the values obtained for the macroalgae and cattle man-
Impact points contributed by the midpoint impact categories for the base scenario.

Impact Category|Cattle Manure (mPt)|Macroalgae (mPt) Fossil fuels|1131.89|1183.61 Resp. inorganics|567.98|602.21 Acidification/Eutrophication|88.42|95.39 Climate change|73.46|75.38 Land use|63.84|66.19 Minerals|39.90|39.76 Carcinogens|25.40|24.32 Ecotoxicity|24.48|23.76 Radiation|0.78|0.77 Resp. organics|0.67|0.70 Ozone Layer|0.03|0.03 Total|2016.85|2112.12

ure biorefineries. Meanwhile, despite the environmental credit of CO2 sequestration allocated to the macroalgae biorefinery, the total impact of the life cycle of this biorefinery is slightly higher than that of the cattle manure biorefinery. The slight difference is mainly due to the transportation energy requirement and carbon gases released during the transportation of the capital goods and process materials. Table 2 shows the single score point contributed by each impact for the base scenario of both biorefineries, in decreasing order of magnitude.

The highest impact is from fossil fuel used for grid energy pro- duction and transportation. The impact contributed by anaerobic decomposition is minimal because the major emissions from this phase are collected in the form of biogas, rather than being released to the environment. The impact from respiratory inorgan- ics is also high due to similar reasons. Respiratory inorganics are the particulate matter produced from the combustion of fossil fuels. This particulate matter includes sulfate and nitrate aerosols that cause respiratory difficulties. The potential of nutrient release from the biorefineries and eventual accumulation in the ground- water and surrounding open water bodies also raises concern for eutrophication, in the case of process failure.

Following these two aforementioned impact categories in terms of decreasing order of impact points are acidification/eutrophica- tion, climate change and land use for both processes. The impacts of both biorefineries are in the following order: Fossil fuel > Respi- ratory inorganics > Acidification and eutrophication > Climate change > Land use > Minerals > Carcinogens > Ecotoxicity > Radia- tion > Respiratory organics > Ozone layer depletion. Acidification and eutrophication are closely related to the particulate matter of sulfate and nitrate from fuel combustion. Sulfate releases toxic SO2 gas which can combine with water bodies in the environment to form sulfuric acid. Acidification can also arise from the CO2 released during the burning of fossil fuels, as ocean pH levels can be decreased by CO2 absorption. Eutrophication, on the other hand, arises from excess nutrient in a water body which can be facilitated by dissolved nitrate. Both biorefineries have impacts related to acidification and eutrophication because the fossil fuels used for transportation and grid electricity production release acidic SO2 and CO2, and eutrophic nitrate during their high-temperature com- bustion. In addition, these gases contribute to climate change. Although macroalgae grow under halophilic conditions and the cattle manure is sourced from the farms, land use still constitutes a relatively considerable impact. This impact evidently arises from the changes to the existing land use, including changes to land availability for commercial processes, tourism, and cultivation of desert crops such as date seeds in Abu Dhabi. The land use change can be direct or indirect; direct change results in direct CO2 emis- sion due to a change from one land use to another while indirect land use occurs when a land that is used previously to grow food crops is now used for biogas production such that the new land for food production results in CO2 emissions (Wicke et al., 2012). In this study, only direct land use change is considered, as no pre- vious cultivation of food on the land is considered.

Although the single score impact of the cattle manure biorefin- ery is lower than that of the macroalgae biorefinery, the energy and water consumed during the biorefining favor macroalgae, as shown in Table 1. Lower consumption of energy and water makes the macroalgae biorefinery to be more eco-friendly in the process- ing and disposal stages. The ratio of the impact points contributed by the macroalgae and cattle manure biorefineries in the process- ing stage is 49:51. In addition, the ratio is 40:60 during the sludge disposal stage because the anaerobic digestion of the cattle manure produces more waste. However, the impact point contributed by transportation is higher for the macroalgae biorefinery because macroalgae needs to be cultivated and the materials required for macroalgae cultivation needs to be transported to the pond site.
Fig. 4. Single score impact points for the three scenarios. CM = cattle manure; M = macroalgae; (1) = base scenario; (2) = scenario 2; (3) = scenario 3. The base scenario is scenario 1.

This makes the animal manure biorefinery to pose lower environ- mental stress when the entire life cycles of the two biorefineries are considered, as the impact of the transportation of materials required for pond construction and macroalgae cultivation out- weighs the environmental gains attributed to the CO2 sequestra- tion by macroalgae.

Scenarios 2 and 3 are assessed and compared with the base sce- nario. Since the major impacts are from fossil fuels and respiratory inorganics, the response of the environmental impacts to a change in transportation fuel is studied in Scenario 2. This scenario involves the recycling of the biogas produced and its use for the land transportation of the inputs (raw materials and capital goods) sourced locally, instead of depending on refined fossil fuels. In Sce- nario 1, all inputs are considered to be transported by fossil fuel- powered transportation systems. Scenario 2 gives lower single score impacts than Scenario 1 for both biorefineries, but the single score impact of the life cycle of the cattle manure biorefinery is still lower than that of macroalgae biorefinery (Fig. 4). There is a more significant reduction in impacts due to fossil fuels, respiratory inor- ganics and acidification/eutrophication resulting from air and water transportation in Scenario 2 because the biogas produced was used for land transportation. Conceptual stages such as pro- cessing and disposal now contribute more significantly to the total environmental impact than the transportation stage in Scenario 2, but the total single score impacts for both biorefineries are lower than those obtained for scenario 1. The single score impact of the cattle manure biorefinery reduces by 11% (to 1795 mPt) and that of macroalgae reduces by 10.5% (to 1890 mPt) in Scenario 2, rela- tive to Scenario 1, as a result of the change in the biogas use. Reduction in the use of fossil fuels for transportation is the most significant contributor to the impact reduction in Scenario 2. The single score impacts become further reduced in Scenario 3 when the source of energy to run the two biorefineries is changed from the natural gas-powered grid electricity to the biogas energy recycled from the biorefineries. In Scenarios 1 and 2, electricity from the grid is considered as the source of energy. The single score impact of the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries reduce by 51.3% (to 983 mPt) and 58.6% (to 875 mPt), respectively, in Sce- nario 3, relative to Scenario 1. This huge reduction is as a result of the replacement of the energy used in the biorefineries, as grid- connected energy is responsible for more than half of the total impacts in Scenario 1. Meanwhile, in Scenario 3, the macroalgae biorefinery has less total impact point than the cattle manure

biorefinery because it requires less energy for biogas production, i.e. 54173.8 MJ, as shown in Table 1. So, the environmental impact of the macroalgae biorefinery can be significantly reduced by recy- cling the bioenergy produced back to the process.

The life cycle impacts of the two biorefineries are also analyzed and compared in terms of the specific emissions released through the combustion of fossil fuels for transportation and grid electricity production. These emissions include nitrogen oxides (NO2 and N20), SO2, and particulate aerosols <2.5 um. For the cattle manure biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission are reduced by 4% and 65% in Scenarios 2 and 3, respectively, with respect to Scenario 1 (Fig. 5A). For the macroalgae biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission reduced by 3.6% and 71%, respectively in Scenarios 2 and 3 (Fig. 5B). Scenario 3 results in a more substantial decrease in nitrogen oxides emission. Also, Scenario 3 reduced the impact due to SO2 emission by 85% and 88% for the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries, respec- tively, as compared to the base scenario.

Unlike the hierarchist method, the individualistic method con- siders all substances that are carcinogenic based on both scientific and non-scientific (historical) data while the egalitarian method considers all substances that can possibly be carcinogenic, regard- less of whether the proofs are available or not (Laleman et al., 2011; Onn and Yusoff, 2010). The Individualistic view agrees with the Hierarchist view but with higher impact points for both biore- fineries (Fig. 5C). However, the egalitarian view results in less total impact points because it allocates more weighting to the environ- mental credit allocated to the CO2 sequestration and reuse of cattle manure. In addition, this view favors macroalgae, as lower total impact point is obtained for the macroalgae biorefinery, because it places more emphasis on the CO2 sequestration of macroalgae.

The impact points of all midpoint and endpoint categories for all scenarios are provided in Appendix A. The endpoint categories are those relating to the environmental impacts at the end of the cause-effect chain, including human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. The most significant inputs (materials, energy and emissions) influencing the impact points of the best case scenario, and the computer screenshot of the Simapro software are also pro- vided in Appendix A.

Meanwhile, there are drivers and barriers towards the imple- mentation of these biorefineries. Policy and decision makers have a major role to play in ensuring that the drivers are properly iden- tified and the barriers are adequately addressed. The practicality of

this comparative assessment also depends on the readiness of the existing infrastructure and cost of biogas production. As at 2012, the total energy consumption in the UAE per capita was 7.5 toe one of the highest in the world. Of the total energy consumed, only 0.13% was contributed by renewable energy. Consequently, the CO2 emission from energy generation in the UAE was 40.31 ton per capita the 6th largest in the world (Guardian, 2011; The World Bank, 2015). Most of the power plants in the UAE are run using natural gas. In addition, natural gas is the fuel for most vehi- cles used for public transport. Therefore, the main driver for the substitution of natural gas with biogas in the UAE is climate change and fossil fuel depletion. However, the high cost of biogas produc- tion in the UAE, as compared to natural gas is a barrier (Belasri et al., 2016). Apart from capital and material transportation costs, the hydrolysis step of the processing stage also contributes signif- icantly to the cost (Yadvika et al., 2004).

There are no reported values for the cost of biogas production from large-scale macroalgae or cattle manure biorefineries in the UAE, as such plants are not available currently. However, the typ- ical levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) from biogas can reach up to USD 0.25/kWh, depending on whether financial support is avail- able or not (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). This cost is not competitive, when it is compared to the cost of other energy sources in the country. The estimated cost of nuclear power in the UAE is USD 0.11/kWh while the cost of natu- ral gas power is USD 0.07/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016) The world record for the cost of solar PV power was established in the UAE in 2016 at USD 0.024/kWh (Binsal Abdul Kader, 2017). The best wind projects are delivering

electricity at USD 0.05/kWh without financial support (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). Coal is expected to generate electric power at USD 0.045/kWh in Dubai once the planned coal plant is operational in 2020 (Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA), 2017), while the cost of electric power production from diesel is USD 0.120.16/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016). Clearly, other available energy sources are cheaper than biogas due to their high conversion efficiencies.

Externalities resulting from the use of natural gas and other fos- sil fuels are also excluded from their current market prices, thereby giving them a competitive economic edge over biogas. Another barrier is the lack of skills and long-term experience. There are very few biorefineries in the country and most of them were commis- sioned recently. In addition, the lack of necessary infrastructure is a critical barrier. To start a full-scale biorefinery in the country, most of the equipment required for the construction would be imported from other countries. As it can be seen from Table 1, cap- ital goods are mainly transported from other countries.

To overcome this barrier, political, social and technological interventions are inevitable. On the political side, the UAE plans to increase the percentage of renewable energy generated through nuclear, solar and biomass energy to 7% by 2020. The UAE also plans to generate 24% of its electricity from clean energy sources by 2021, according to the agreement signed by the country at the COP21 United Nations Climate Change Conference in December 2015 (Embassy of the United Arab Emirates, 2016). The economic hub of the country, i.e. Dubai emirate, hopes to increase the per- centage of renewable energy generated in the emirate to 75% by

2050 (Emirates News Agency, 2016). For biogas production, there are plans to introduce a feed-in-tariff that offers a fixed attractive price in order to create a market for biogas and recover the cost of production. Biogas production quotas would be assigned to pro- ducers and inventors to ensure that the market share required is achieved, as part of the broader renewable energy policy. Another political intervention is the practice of contract biddings or conces- sion for bioenergy projects. Direct subsidies in form of tax rebates have been introduced to lessen the risks associated with the high initial (capital) cost of renewable energy plants (Abdmouleh et al., 2015; Mezher et al., 2012).

The recent downtrend in the prices of fossil-based fuels globally has also encouraged political interventions in the implementation of biogas projects. The declining crude oil prices have led to falling state revenues and triggered a series of energy price reforms recently. With crude oil priced at less than USD 50/bbl currently, the wide price gap between crude oil and renewables has been lessened. As a result of this, the national oil company (TAQA) has initiated the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) of UAEs first waste-to-energy plant that is expected to produce 100 MW of electric power, which is enough to power 20,000 houses (Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (TAQA), 2017, Jamil et al., 2016).

On the social side, more awareness campaigns are required to encourage energy stakeholders to utilize macroalgae and cattle manure for large-scale biogas production. The marginal cost at lar- ger scales might eventually bring down the cost of biogas produc- tion. On the technological side, the critical infrastructure needed to ensure commercial deployment of biogas would have to be pro- vided. Scientific research on enhanced macroalgal conversion tech- nologies involving molecular bioengineering (Jung et al., 2013), supercritical water gasification (Patel et al., 2016), and the utiliza- tion of atmospheric CO2 captured in geological reservoirs for algal growth (Moreira and Pires, 2016) should be intensified.
35: For the base scenario, the life cycle (single score total impact) of biogas production from macroalgae is 2112 mPt which is slightly higher than 2017 mPt obtained for biogas production from cattle manure. These impact points are obtained from the inventory data supplied as inputs to Simapro 7.3.3 for both biorefineries. The energy and material usage and environmental releases by the inventories are converted to environmental impact points using the in-built characterization factor for each inventory in Simapro 7.3.3. These are relatively low impact points because the process- ing of biomass to biogas is known to have low environmental impact. This is because the gases produced are trapped, rather than released to the environment. It has been reported recently that the single score impact points of gasoline and kerosene production via petroleum refining are 11,100 mPt and 4830 mPt for every m³ of each fuel (Alanbari et al., 2016). These are values that are much higher than the values obtained for the macroalgae and cattle man-
Impact points contributed by the midpoint impact categories for the base scenario.

Impact Category|Cattle Manure (mPt)|Macroalgae (mPt) Fossil fuels|1131.89|1183.61 Resp. inorganics|567.98|602.21 Acidification/Eutrophication|88.42|95.39 Climate change|73.46|75.38 Land use|63.84|66.19 Minerals|39.90|39.76 Carcinogens|25.40|24.32 Ecotoxicity|24.48|23.76 Radiation|0.78|0.77 Resp. organics|0.67|0.70 Ozone Layer|0.03|0.03 Total|2016.85|2112.12

ure biorefineries. Meanwhile, despite the environmental credit of CO2 sequestration allocated to the macroalgae biorefinery, the total impact of the life cycle of this biorefinery is slightly higher than that of the cattle manure biorefinery. The slight difference is mainly due to the transportation energy requirement and carbon gases released during the transportation of the capital goods and process materials. Table 2 shows the single score point contributed by each impact for the base scenario of both biorefineries, in decreasing order of magnitude.

The highest impact is from fossil fuel used for grid energy pro- duction and transportation. The impact contributed by anaerobic decomposition is minimal because the major emissions from this phase are collected in the form of biogas, rather than being released to the environment. The impact from respiratory inorgan- ics is also high due to similar reasons. Respiratory inorganics are the particulate matter produced from the combustion of fossil fuels. This particulate matter includes sulfate and nitrate aerosols that cause respiratory difficulties. The potential of nutrient release from the biorefineries and eventual accumulation in the ground- water and surrounding open water bodies also raises concern for eutrophication, in the case of process failure.

Following these two aforementioned impact categories in terms of decreasing order of impact points are acidification/eutrophica- tion, climate change and land use for both processes. The impacts of both biorefineries are in the following order: Fossil fuel > Respi- ratory inorganics > Acidification and eutrophication > Climate change > Land use > Minerals > Carcinogens > Ecotoxicity > Radia- tion > Respiratory organics > Ozone layer depletion. Acidification and eutrophication are closely related to the particulate matter of sulfate and nitrate from fuel combustion. Sulfate releases toxic SO2 gas which can combine with water bodies in the environment to form sulfuric acid. Acidification can also arise from the CO2 released during the burning of fossil fuels, as ocean pH levels can be decreased by CO2 absorption. Eutrophication, on the other hand, arises from excess nutrient in a water body which can be facilitated by dissolved nitrate. Both biorefineries have impacts related to acidification and eutrophication because the fossil fuels used for transportation and grid electricity production release acidic SO2 and CO2, and eutrophic nitrate during their high-temperature com- bustion. In addition, these gases contribute to climate change. Although macroalgae grow under halophilic conditions and the cattle manure is sourced from the farms, land use still constitutes a relatively considerable impact. This impact evidently arises from the changes to the existing land use, including changes to land availability for commercial processes, tourism, and cultivation of desert crops such as date seeds in Abu Dhabi. The land use change can be direct or indirect; direct change results in direct CO2 emis- sion due to a change from one land use to another while indirect land use occurs when a land that is used previously to grow food crops is now used for biogas production such that the new land for food production results in CO2 emissions (Wicke et al., 2012). In this study, only direct land use change is considered, as no pre- vious cultivation of food on the land is considered.

Although the single score impact of the cattle manure biorefin- ery is lower than that of the macroalgae biorefinery, the energy and water consumed during the biorefining favor macroalgae, as shown in Table 1. Lower consumption of energy and water makes the macroalgae biorefinery to be more eco-friendly in the process- ing and disposal stages. The ratio of the impact points contributed by the macroalgae and cattle manure biorefineries in the process- ing stage is 49:51. In addition, the ratio is 40:60 during the sludge disposal stage because the anaerobic digestion of the cattle manure produces more waste. However, the impact point contributed by transportation is higher for the macroalgae biorefinery because macroalgae needs to be cultivated and the materials required for macroalgae cultivation needs to be transported to the pond site.
Fig. 4. Single score impact points for the three scenarios. CM = cattle manure; M = macroalgae; (1) = base scenario; (2) = scenario 2; (3) = scenario 3. The base scenario is scenario 1.

This makes the animal manure biorefinery to pose lower environ- mental stress when the entire life cycles of the two biorefineries are considered, as the impact of the transportation of materials required for pond construction and macroalgae cultivation out- weighs the environmental gains attributed to the CO2 sequestra- tion by macroalgae.

Scenarios 2 and 3 are assessed and compared with the base sce- nario. Since the major impacts are from fossil fuels and respiratory inorganics, the response of the environmental impacts to a change in transportation fuel is studied in Scenario 2. This scenario involves the recycling of the biogas produced and its use for the land transportation of the inputs (raw materials and capital goods) sourced locally, instead of depending on refined fossil fuels. In Sce- nario 1, all inputs are considered to be transported by fossil fuel- powered transportation systems. Scenario 2 gives lower single score impacts than Scenario 1 for both biorefineries, but the single score impact of the life cycle of the cattle manure biorefinery is still lower than that of macroalgae biorefinery (Fig. 4). There is a more significant reduction in impacts due to fossil fuels, respiratory inor- ganics and acidification/eutrophication resulting from air and water transportation in Scenario 2 because the biogas produced was used for land transportation. Conceptual stages such as pro- cessing and disposal now contribute more significantly to the total environmental impact than the transportation stage in Scenario 2, but the total single score impacts for both biorefineries are lower than those obtained for scenario 1. The single score impact of the cattle manure biorefinery reduces by 11% (to 1795 mPt) and that of macroalgae reduces by 10.5% (to 1890 mPt) in Scenario 2, rela- tive to Scenario 1, as a result of the change in the biogas use. Reduction in the use of fossil fuels for transportation is the most significant contributor to the impact reduction in Scenario 2. The single score impacts become further reduced in Scenario 3 when the source of energy to run the two biorefineries is changed from the natural gas-powered grid electricity to the biogas energy recycled from the biorefineries. In Scenarios 1 and 2, electricity from the grid is considered as the source of energy. The single score impact of the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries reduce by 51.3% (to 983 mPt) and 58.6% (to 875 mPt), respectively, in Sce- nario 3, relative to Scenario 1. This huge reduction is as a result of the replacement of the energy used in the biorefineries, as grid- connected energy is responsible for more than half of the total impacts in Scenario 1. Meanwhile, in Scenario 3, the macroalgae biorefinery has less total impact point than the cattle manure

biorefinery because it requires less energy for biogas production, i.e. 54173.8 MJ, as shown in Table 1. So, the environmental impact of the macroalgae biorefinery can be significantly reduced by recy- cling the bioenergy produced back to the process.

The life cycle impacts of the two biorefineries are also analyzed and compared in terms of the specific emissions released through the combustion of fossil fuels for transportation and grid electricity production. These emissions include nitrogen oxides (NO2 and N20), SO2, and particulate aerosols <2.5 um. For the cattle manure biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission are reduced by 4% and 65% in Scenarios 2 and 3, respectively, with respect to Scenario 1 (Fig. 5A). For the macroalgae biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission reduced by 3.6% and 71%, respectively in Scenarios 2 and 3 (Fig. 5B). Scenario 3 results in a more substantial decrease in nitrogen oxides emission. Also, Scenario 3 reduced the impact due to SO2 emission by 85% and 88% for the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries, respec- tively, as compared to the base scenario.

Unlike the hierarchist method, the individualistic method con- siders all substances that are carcinogenic based on both scientific and non-scientific (historical) data while the egalitarian method considers all substances that can possibly be carcinogenic, regard- less of whether the proofs are available or not (Laleman et al., 2011; Onn and Yusoff, 2010). The Individualistic view agrees with the Hierarchist view but with higher impact points for both biore- fineries (Fig. 5C). However, the egalitarian view results in less total impact points because it allocates more weighting to the environ- mental credit allocated to the CO2 sequestration and reuse of cattle manure. In addition, this view favors macroalgae, as lower total impact point is obtained for the macroalgae biorefinery, because it places more emphasis on the CO2 sequestration of macroalgae.

The impact points of all midpoint and endpoint categories for all scenarios are provided in Appendix A. The endpoint categories are those relating to the environmental impacts at the end of the cause-effect chain, including human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. The most significant inputs (materials, energy and emissions) influencing the impact points of the best case scenario, and the computer screenshot of the Simapro software are also pro- vided in Appendix A.

Meanwhile, there are drivers and barriers towards the imple- mentation of these biorefineries. Policy and decision makers have a major role to play in ensuring that the drivers are properly iden- tified and the barriers are adequately addressed. The practicality of

Fig. 5. Emissions released from the life cycles of the (A) cattle manure and (B) macroalgae biorefineries. (C) Impact points based on Hierarchist (Eco99-H), Egalitarian (Eco99- E), and Individualistic (Eco99-I) views for cattle manure (CM) and macroalgae (M) biorefineries.

this comparative assessment also depends on the readiness of the existing infrastructure and cost of biogas production. As at 2012, the total energy consumption in the UAE per capita was 7.5 toe one of the highest in the world. Of the total energy consumed, only 0.13% was contributed by renewable energy. Consequently, the CO2 emission from energy generation in the UAE was 40.31 ton per capita the 6th largest in the world (Guardian, 2011; The World Bank, 2015). Most of the power plants in the UAE are run using natural gas. In addition, natural gas is the fuel for most vehi- cles used for public transport. Therefore, the main driver for the substitution of natural gas with biogas in the UAE is climate change and fossil fuel depletion. However, the high cost of biogas produc- tion in the UAE, as compared to natural gas is a barrier (Belasri et al., 2016). Apart from capital and material transportation costs, the hydrolysis step of the processing stage also contributes signif- icantly to the cost (Yadvika et al., 2004).

There are no reported values for the cost of biogas production from large-scale macroalgae or cattle manure biorefineries in the UAE, as such plants are not available currently. However, the typ- ical levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) from biogas can reach up to USD 0.25/kWh, depending on whether financial support is avail- able or not (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). This cost is not competitive, when it is compared to the cost of other energy sources in the country. The estimated cost of nuclear power in the UAE is USD 0.11/kWh while the cost of natu- ral gas power is USD 0.07/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016) The world record for the cost of solar PV power was established in the UAE in 2016 at USD 0.024/kWh (Binsal Abdul Kader, 2017). The best wind projects are delivering

electricity at USD 0.05/kWh without financial support (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). Coal is expected to generate electric power at USD 0.045/kWh in Dubai once the planned coal plant is operational in 2020 (Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA), 2017), while the cost of electric power production from diesel is USD 0.120.16/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016). Clearly, other available energy sources are cheaper than biogas due to their high conversion efficiencies.

Externalities resulting from the use of natural gas and other fos- sil fuels are also excluded from their current market prices, thereby giving them a competitive economic edge over biogas. Another barrier is the lack of skills and long-term experience. There are very few biorefineries in the country and most of them were commis- sioned recently. In addition, the lack of necessary infrastructure is a critical barrier. To start a full-scale biorefinery in the country, most of the equipment required for the construction would be imported from other countries. As it can be seen from Table 1, cap- ital goods are mainly transported from other countries.

To overcome this barrier, political, social and technological interventions are inevitable. On the political side, the UAE plans to increase the percentage of renewable energy generated through nuclear, solar and biomass energy to 7% by 2020. The UAE also plans to generate 24% of its electricity from clean energy sources by 2021, according to the agreement signed by the country at the COP21 United Nations Climate Change Conference in December 2015 (Embassy of the United Arab Emirates, 2016). The economic hub of the country, i.e. Dubai emirate, hopes to increase the per- centage of renewable energy generated in the emirate to 75% by

2050 (Emirates News Agency, 2016). For biogas production, there are plans to introduce a feed-in-tariff that offers a fixed attractive price in order to create a market for biogas and recover the cost of production. Biogas production quotas would be assigned to pro- ducers and inventors to ensure that the market share required is achieved, as part of the broader renewable energy policy. Another political intervention is the practice of contract biddings or conces- sion for bioenergy projects. Direct subsidies in form of tax rebates have been introduced to lessen the risks associated with the high initial (capital) cost of renewable energy plants (Abdmouleh et al., 2015; Mezher et al., 2012).

The recent downtrend in the prices of fossil-based fuels globally has also encouraged political interventions in the implementation of biogas projects. The declining crude oil prices have led to falling state revenues and triggered a series of energy price reforms recently. With crude oil priced at less than USD 50/bbl currently, the wide price gap between crude oil and renewables has been lessened. As a result of this, the national oil company (TAQA) has initiated the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) of UAEs first waste-to-energy plant that is expected to produce 100 MW of electric power, which is enough to power 20,000 houses (Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (TAQA), 2017, Jamil et al., 2016).

On the social side, more awareness campaigns are required to encourage energy stakeholders to utilize macroalgae and cattle manure for large-scale biogas production. The marginal cost at lar- ger scales might eventually bring down the cost of biogas produc- tion. On the technological side, the critical infrastructure needed to ensure commercial deployment of biogas would have to be pro- vided. Scientific research on enhanced macroalgal conversion tech- nologies involving molecular bioengineering (Jung et al., 2013), supercritical water gasification (Patel et al., 2016), and the utiliza- tion of atmospheric CO2 captured in geological reservoirs for algal growth (Moreira and Pires, 2016) should be intensified.

These impact points are obtained from the inventory data supplied as inputs to Simapro 7.3.3 for both biorefineries. The energy and material usage and environmental releases by the inventories are converted to environmental impact points using the in-built characterization factor for each inventory in Simapro 7.3.3. These are relatively low impact points because the process- ing of biomass to biogas is known to have low environmental impact. This is because the gases produced are trapped, rather than released to the environment. It has been reported recently that the single score impact points of gasoline and kerosene production via petroleum refining are 11,100 mPt and 4830 mPt for every m³ of each fuel (Alanbari et al., 2016). These are values that are much higher than the values obtained for the macroalgae and cattle man-
Impact points contributed by the midpoint impact categories for the base scenario.

Impact Category|Cattle Manure (mPt)|Macroalgae (mPt) Fossil fuels|1131.89|1183.61 Resp. inorganics|567.98|602.21 Acidification/Eutrophication|88.42|95.39 Climate change|73.46|75.38 Land use|63.84|66.19 Minerals|39.90|39.76 Carcinogens|25.40|24.32 Ecotoxicity|24.48|23.76 Radiation|0.78|0.77 Resp. organics|0.67|0.70 Ozone Layer|0.03|0.03 Total|2016.85|2112.12

ure biorefineries. Meanwhile, despite the environmental credit of CO2 sequestration allocated to the macroalgae biorefinery, the total impact of the life cycle of this biorefinery is slightly higher than that of the cattle manure biorefinery. The slight difference is mainly due to the transportation energy requirement and carbon gases released during the transportation of the capital goods and process materials. Table 2 shows the single score point contributed by each impact for the base scenario of both biorefineries, in decreasing order of magnitude.

The highest impact is from fossil fuel used for grid energy pro- duction and transportation. The impact contributed by anaerobic decomposition is minimal because the major emissions from this phase are collected in the form of biogas, rather than being released to the environment. The impact from respiratory inorgan- ics is also high due to similar reasons. Respiratory inorganics are the particulate matter produced from the combustion of fossil fuels. This particulate matter includes sulfate and nitrate aerosols that cause respiratory difficulties. The potential of nutrient release from the biorefineries and eventual accumulation in the ground- water and surrounding open water bodies also raises concern for eutrophication, in the case of process failure.

Following these two aforementioned impact categories in terms of decreasing order of impact points are acidification/eutrophica- tion, climate change and land use for both processes. The impacts of both biorefineries are in the following order: Fossil fuel > Respi- ratory inorganics > Acidification and eutrophication > Climate change > Land use > Minerals > Carcinogens > Ecotoxicity > Radia- tion > Respiratory organics > Ozone layer depletion. Acidification and eutrophication are closely related to the particulate matter of sulfate and nitrate from fuel combustion. Sulfate releases toxic SO2 gas which can combine with water bodies in the environment to form sulfuric acid. Acidification can also arise from the CO2 released during the burning of fossil fuels, as ocean pH levels can be decreased by CO2 absorption. Eutrophication, on the other hand, arises from excess nutrient in a water body which can be facilitated by dissolved nitrate. Both biorefineries have impacts related to acidification and eutrophication because the fossil fuels used for transportation and grid electricity production release acidic SO2 and CO2, and eutrophic nitrate during their high-temperature com- bustion. In addition, these gases contribute to climate change. Although macroalgae grow under halophilic conditions and the cattle manure is sourced from the farms, land use still constitutes a relatively considerable impact. This impact evidently arises from the changes to the existing land use, including changes to land availability for commercial processes, tourism, and cultivation of desert crops such as date seeds in Abu Dhabi. The land use change can be direct or indirect; direct change results in direct CO2 emis- sion due to a change from one land use to another while indirect land use occurs when a land that is used previously to grow food crops is now used for biogas production such that the new land for food production results in CO2 emissions (Wicke et al., 2012). In this study, only direct land use change is considered, as no pre- vious cultivation of food on the land is considered.

Although the single score impact of the cattle manure biorefin- ery is lower than that of the macroalgae biorefinery, the energy and water consumed during the biorefining favor macroalgae, as shown in Table 1. Lower consumption of energy and water makes the macroalgae biorefinery to be more eco-friendly in the process- ing and disposal stages. The ratio of the impact points contributed by the macroalgae and cattle manure biorefineries in the process- ing stage is 49:51. In addition, the ratio is 40:60 during the sludge disposal stage because the anaerobic digestion of the cattle manure produces more waste. However, the impact point contributed by transportation is higher for the macroalgae biorefinery because macroalgae needs to be cultivated and the materials required for macroalgae cultivation needs to be transported to the pond site.
Fig. 4. Single score impact points for the three scenarios. CM = cattle manure; M = macroalgae; (1) = base scenario; (2) = scenario 2; (3) = scenario 3. The base scenario is scenario 1.

This makes the animal manure biorefinery to pose lower environ- mental stress when the entire life cycles of the two biorefineries are considered, as the impact of the transportation of materials required for pond construction and macroalgae cultivation out- weighs the environmental gains attributed to the CO2 sequestra- tion by macroalgae.

Scenarios 2 and 3 are assessed and compared with the base sce- nario. Since the major impacts are from fossil fuels and respiratory inorganics, the response of the environmental impacts to a change in transportation fuel is studied in Scenario 2. This scenario involves the recycling of the biogas produced and its use for the land transportation of the inputs (raw materials and capital goods) sourced locally, instead of depending on refined fossil fuels. In Sce- nario 1, all inputs are considered to be transported by fossil fuel- powered transportation systems. Scenario 2 gives lower single score impacts than Scenario 1 for both biorefineries, but the single score impact of the life cycle of the cattle manure biorefinery is still lower than that of macroalgae biorefinery (Fig. 4). There is a more significant reduction in impacts due to fossil fuels, respiratory inor- ganics and acidification/eutrophication resulting from air and water transportation in Scenario 2 because the biogas produced was used for land transportation. Conceptual stages such as pro- cessing and disposal now contribute more significantly to the total environmental impact than the transportation stage in Scenario 2, but the total single score impacts for both biorefineries are lower than those obtained for scenario 1. The single score impact of the cattle manure biorefinery reduces by 11% (to 1795 mPt) and that of macroalgae reduces by 10.5% (to 1890 mPt) in Scenario 2, rela- tive to Scenario 1, as a result of the change in the biogas use. Reduction in the use of fossil fuels for transportation is the most significant contributor to the impact reduction in Scenario 2. The single score impacts become further reduced in Scenario 3 when the source of energy to run the two biorefineries is changed from the natural gas-powered grid electricity to the biogas energy recycled from the biorefineries. In Scenarios 1 and 2, electricity from the grid is considered as the source of energy. The single score impact of the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries reduce by 51.3% (to 983 mPt) and 58.6% (to 875 mPt), respectively, in Sce- nario 3, relative to Scenario 1. This huge reduction is as a result of the replacement of the energy used in the biorefineries, as grid- connected energy is responsible for more than half of the total impacts in Scenario 1. Meanwhile, in Scenario 3, the macroalgae biorefinery has less total impact point than the cattle manure

biorefinery because it requires less energy for biogas production, i.e. 54173.8 MJ, as shown in Table 1. So, the environmental impact of the macroalgae biorefinery can be significantly reduced by recy- cling the bioenergy produced back to the process.

The life cycle impacts of the two biorefineries are also analyzed and compared in terms of the specific emissions released through the combustion of fossil fuels for transportation and grid electricity production. These emissions include nitrogen oxides (NO2 and N20), SO2, and particulate aerosols <2.5 um. For the cattle manure biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission are reduced by 4% and 65% in Scenarios 2 and 3, respectively, with respect to Scenario 1 (Fig. 5A). For the macroalgae biorefinery, the impacts due to nitrogen oxides emission reduced by 3.6% and 71%, respectively in Scenarios 2 and 3 (Fig. 5B). Scenario 3 results in a more substantial decrease in nitrogen oxides emission. Also, Scenario 3 reduced the impact due to SO2 emission by 85% and 88% for the cattle manure and macroalgae biorefineries, respec- tively, as compared to the base scenario.

Unlike the hierarchist method, the individualistic method con- siders all substances that are carcinogenic based on both scientific and non-scientific (historical) data while the egalitarian method considers all substances that can possibly be carcinogenic, regard- less of whether the proofs are available or not (Laleman et al., 2011; Onn and Yusoff, 2010). The Individualistic view agrees with the Hierarchist view but with higher impact points for both biore- fineries (Fig. 5C). However, the egalitarian view results in less total impact points because it allocates more weighting to the environ- mental credit allocated to the CO2 sequestration and reuse of cattle manure. In addition, this view favors macroalgae, as lower total impact point is obtained for the macroalgae biorefinery, because it places more emphasis on the CO2 sequestration of macroalgae.

The impact points of all midpoint and endpoint categories for all scenarios are provided in Appendix A. The endpoint categories are those relating to the environmental impacts at the end of the cause-effect chain, including human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. The most significant inputs (materials, energy and emissions) influencing the impact points of the best case scenario, and the computer screenshot of the Simapro software are also pro- vided in Appendix A.

The Individualistic view agrees with the Hierarchist view but with higher impact points for both biore- fineries (Fig. 5C). However, the egalitarian view results in less total impact points because it allocates more weighting to the environ- mental credit allocated to the CO2 sequestration and reuse of cattle manure. In addition, this view favors macroalgae, as lower total impact point is obtained for the macroalgae biorefinery, because it places more emphasis on the CO2 sequestration of macroalgae.

The impact points of all midpoint and endpoint categories for all scenarios are provided in Appendix A. The endpoint categories are those relating to the environmental impacts at the end of the cause-effect chain, including human health, ecosystem quality, and resources. The most significant inputs (materials, energy and emissions) influencing the impact points of the best case scenario, and the computer screenshot of the Simapro software are also pro- vided in Appendix A.

Meanwhile, there are drivers and barriers towards the imple- mentation of these biorefineries. Policy and decision makers have a major role to play in ensuring that the drivers are properly iden- tified and the barriers are adequately addressed. The practicality of

Fig. 5. Emissions released from the life cycles of the (A) cattle manure and (B) macroalgae biorefineries. (C) Impact points based on Hierarchist (Eco99-H), Egalitarian (Eco99- E), and Individualistic (Eco99-I) views for cattle manure (CM) and macroalgae (M) biorefineries.

this comparative assessment also depends on the readiness of the existing infrastructure and cost of biogas production. As at 2012, the total energy consumption in the UAE per capita was 7.5 toe one of the highest in the world. Of the total energy consumed, only 0.13% was contributed by renewable energy. Consequently, the CO2 emission from energy generation in the UAE was 40.31 ton per capita the 6th largest in the world (Guardian, 2011; The World Bank, 2015). Most of the power plants in the UAE are run using natural gas. In addition, natural gas is the fuel for most vehi- cles used for public transport. Therefore, the main driver for the substitution of natural gas with biogas in the UAE is climate change and fossil fuel depletion. However, the high cost of biogas produc- tion in the UAE, as compared to natural gas is a barrier (Belasri et al., 2016). Apart from capital and material transportation costs, the hydrolysis step of the processing stage also contributes signif- icantly to the cost (Yadvika et al., 2004).

There are no reported values for the cost of biogas production from large-scale macroalgae or cattle manure biorefineries in the UAE, as such plants are not available currently. However, the typ- ical levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) from biogas can reach up to USD 0.25/kWh, depending on whether financial support is avail- able or not (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). This cost is not competitive, when it is compared to the cost of other energy sources in the country. The estimated cost of nuclear power in the UAE is USD 0.11/kWh while the cost of natu- ral gas power is USD 0.07/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016) The world record for the cost of solar PV power was established in the UAE in 2016 at USD 0.024/kWh (Binsal Abdul Kader, 2017). The best wind projects are delivering

electricity at USD 0.05/kWh without financial support (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2015). Coal is expected to generate electric power at USD 0.045/kWh in Dubai once the planned coal plant is operational in 2020 (Dubai Electricity and Water Authority (DEWA), 2017), while the cost of electric power production from diesel is USD 0.120.16/kWh (International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), 2016). Clearly, other available energy sources are cheaper than biogas due to their high conversion efficiencies.

Externalities resulting from the use of natural gas and other fos- sil fuels are also excluded from their current market prices, thereby giving them a competitive economic edge over biogas. Another barrier is the lack of skills and long-term experience. There are very few biorefineries in the country and most of them were commis- sioned recently. In addition, the lack of necessary infrastructure is a critical barrier. To start a full-scale biorefinery in the country, most of the equipment required for the construction would be imported from other countries. As it can be seen from Table 1, cap- ital goods are mainly transported from other countries.

To overcome this barrier, political, social and technological interventions are inevitable. On the political side, the UAE plans to increase the percentage of renewable energy generated through nuclear, solar and biomass energy to 7% by 2020. The UAE also plans to generate 24% of its electricity from clean energy sources by 2021, according to the agreement signed by the country at the COP21 United Nations Climate Change Conference in December 2015 (Embassy of the United Arab Emirates, 2016). The economic hub of the country, i.e. Dubai emirate, hopes to increase the per- centage of renewable energy generated in the emirate to 75% by

2050 (Emirates News Agency, 2016). For biogas production, there are plans to introduce a feed-in-tariff that offers a fixed attractive price in order to create a market for biogas and recover the cost of production. Biogas production quotas would be assigned to pro- ducers and inventors to ensure that the market share required is achieved, as part of the broader renewable energy policy. Another political intervention is the practice of contract biddings or conces- sion for bioenergy projects. Direct subsidies in form of tax rebates have been introduced to lessen the risks associated with the high initial (capital) cost of renewable energy plants (Abdmouleh et al., 2015; Mezher et al., 2012).

The recent downtrend in the prices of fossil-based fuels globally has also encouraged political interventions in the implementation of biogas projects. The declining crude oil prices have led to falling state revenues and triggered a series of energy price reforms recently. With crude oil priced at less than USD 50/bbl currently, the wide price gap between crude oil and renewables has been lessened. As a result of this, the national oil company (TAQA) has initiated the engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) of UAEs first waste-to-energy plant that is expected to produce 100 MW of electric power, which is enough to power 20,000 houses (Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (TAQA), 2017, Jamil et al., 2016).

On the social side, more awareness campaigns are required to encourage energy stakeholders to utilize macroalgae and cattle manure for large-scale biogas production. The marginal cost at lar- ger scales might eventually bring down the cost of biogas produc- tion. On the technological side, the critical infrastructure needed to ensure commercial deployment of biogas would have to be pro- vided. Scientific research on enhanced macroalgal conversion tech- nologies involving molecular bioengineering (Jung et al., 2013), supercritical water gasification (Patel et al., 2016), and the utiliza- tion of atmospheric CO2 captured in geological reservoirs for algal growth (Moreira and Pires, 2016) should be intensified.
